Biology.teks_description: Compare and contrast prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, including their complexity
Biology.prompt: This topic covers cell structure and function for Biology students (TEKS B.5(B)). Students should understand the differences between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, including complexity, organelles, and organization. Key concepts: cell membrane structure (phospholipid bilayer, fluid mosaic model), nucleus and genetic material, mitochondria and energy production, ribosomes and protein synthesis, endoplasmic reticulum (rough and smooth), Golgi apparatus, lysosomes, and cytoskeleton. Compare prokaryotic cells (no membrane-bound organelles, circular DNA, smaller) with eukaryotic cells (membrane-bound organelles, linear chromosomes, larger). Plant cells have additional structures: cell wall, chloroplasts, and large central vacuole. Vocabulary: organelle, cytoplasm, nucleoid, plasmid, endosymbiotic theory, compartmentalization. Students should connect cell structure to function and understand how compartmentalization allows for specialized chemical environments. Common misconceptions: all cells look the same; prokaryotes are primitive and simple (they are highly adapted); plant cells do not have mitochondria (they do).

Biology.teks_description: Investigate homeostasis through molecular cellular transport
Biology.prompt: This topic covers cell transport mechanisms for Biology students (TEKS B.5(C)). Students should understand how cells maintain homeostasis through molecular transport across membranes. Key concepts: passive transport (diffusion, osmosis, facilitated diffusion) requires no energy and moves substances down concentration gradients; active transport (sodium-potassium pump, endocytosis, exocytosis) requires ATP and moves substances against concentration gradients. Osmotic conditions: hypertonic (cell shrinks), hypotonic (cell swells), isotonic (equilibrium). Understand the selectively permeable nature of the cell membrane and how size, polarity, and charge affect transport. Vocabulary: concentration gradient, equilibrium, osmosis, plasmolysis, lysis, turgor pressure, vesicle, pinocytosis, phagocytosis, channel protein, carrier protein. Students should be able to predict cell behavior in different solutions and explain how transport maintains cellular homeostasis. Common misconceptions: osmosis only involves water moving into cells; diffusion stops when molecules are evenly distributed (it reaches dynamic equilibrium).

Biology.teks_description: Explain matter conservation and energy transfer during photosynthesis and cellular respiration
Biology.prompt: This topic covers photosynthesis and cellular respiration for Biology students (TEKS B.11(A)). Students should understand how energy flows through living systems via these complementary processes. Photosynthesis: 6CO2 + 6H2O + light energy → C6H12O6 + 6O2. Occurs in chloroplasts; light-dependent reactions (thylakoid membranes) produce ATP and NADPH; Calvin cycle (stroma) fixes carbon into glucose. Cellular respiration: C6H12O6 + 6O2 → 6CO2 + 6H2O + ATP. Occurs in mitochondria; glycolysis (cytoplasm), Krebs cycle (matrix), electron transport chain (inner membrane). Key concepts: conservation of matter, energy transformation (light to chemical to usable ATP), role of enzymes, ATP as energy currency. Vocabulary: chlorophyll, thylakoid, stroma, glycolysis, pyruvate, acetyl-CoA, NADH, FADH2, oxidative phosphorylation, chemiosmosis, anaerobic respiration, fermentation. Common misconceptions: plants only photosynthesize (they also respire); photosynthesis and respiration are exact opposites (they differ in pathways and efficiency).

Biology.teks_description: Explain the cell cycle significance, including DNA replication and mitosis stages
Biology.prompt: This topic covers cellular reproduction for Biology students (TEKS B.6(A)). Students should understand the cell cycle and both mitosis and meiosis. Cell cycle phases: G1 (growth), S (DNA synthesis), G2 (preparation), M (mitosis). Mitosis produces two genetically identical diploid daughter cells for growth and repair; phases: prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase, cytokinesis. Meiosis produces four genetically unique haploid gametes; includes two divisions (meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes, meiosis II separates sister chromatids). Key concepts: crossing over and independent assortment increase genetic diversity, checkpoints regulate the cell cycle, cancer results from uncontrolled cell division. Vocabulary: chromatin, chromosome, centromere, spindle fiber, homologous chromosomes, tetrad, synapsis, chiasma, diploid (2n), haploid (n), gamete, somatic cell. Common misconceptions: mitosis and meiosis produce the same type of cells; crossing over happens in mitosis.

Biology.teks_description: Identify DNA components and how nucleotide sequences specify traits
Biology.prompt: This topic covers DNA structure and replication for Biology students (TEKS B.7(A)). Students should understand the double helix structure of DNA, including the sugar-phosphate backbone and complementary base pairing (adenine-thymine, guanine-cytosine). Key concepts: DNA as the molecule of heredity, the semi-conservative model of replication, roles of enzymes (helicase unwinds, primase adds RNA primer, DNA polymerase adds nucleotides, ligase seals gaps), leading and lagging strands, and Okazaki fragments. Students should understand why accurate replication is essential for cell division and how errors (mutations) can occur. Nucleotide structure: deoxyribose sugar, phosphate group, nitrogenous base (purines: A, G; pyrimidines: C, T). Vocabulary: nucleotide, base pair, complementary, antiparallel, replication fork, helicase, polymerase, ligase, semi-conservative, template strand, 5' to 3' direction. Common misconceptions: DNA replication produces two completely new molecules (it is semi-conservative); all mutations are harmful (many are neutral).

Biology.teks_description: Describe gene expression and protein synthesis using models
Biology.prompt: This topic covers RNA and protein synthesis for Biology students (TEKS B.7(B)). Students should understand the central dogma: DNA → RNA → Protein. Transcription: DNA is used as a template to produce mRNA in the nucleus; RNA polymerase reads the template strand 3' to 5' and builds mRNA 5' to 3'. RNA uses uracil instead of thymine. mRNA processing: 5' cap, poly-A tail, splicing of introns. Translation: ribosomes read mRNA codons (three-nucleotide sequences) and tRNA brings corresponding amino acids; occurs at ribosomes in the cytoplasm. Key concepts: codon-anticodon pairing, start codon (AUG/methionine), stop codons (UAA, UAG, UGA), genetic code is universal and degenerate. Types of RNA: mRNA (messenger), tRNA (transfer), rRNA (ribosomal). Vocabulary: transcription, translation, codon, anticodon, amino acid, polypeptide, exon, intron, promoter, terminator. Common misconceptions: DNA leaves the nucleus; each codon codes for a unique amino acid (the code is degenerate).

Biology.teks_description: Predict genetic outcomes using monohybrid and dihybrid crosses
Biology.prompt: This topic covers Mendelian genetics for Biology students (TEKS B.8(B)). Students should understand Gregor Mendel's laws of inheritance and how to predict genetic outcomes. Law of Segregation: each organism has two alleles for each trait that separate during gamete formation. Law of Independent Assortment: genes on different chromosomes sort independently. Key concepts: dominant vs. recessive alleles, genotype vs. phenotype, homozygous vs. heterozygous, Punnett squares for monohybrid (one trait) and dihybrid (two traits) crosses, probability ratios (3:1 for monohybrid F2, 9:3:3:1 for dihybrid F2). Test crosses identify unknown genotypes. Vocabulary: allele, locus, gene, trait, P generation, F1 generation, F2 generation, gamete, hybrid, true-breeding, carrier. Students should be able to set up and solve Punnett squares, calculate phenotypic and genotypic ratios, and apply probability to genetics problems. Common misconceptions: dominant traits are more common in populations; blending inheritance is correct.

Biology.teks_description: Predict genetic outcomes including non-Mendelian traits such as incomplete dominance and codominance
Biology.prompt: This topic covers non-Mendelian genetics for Biology students (TEKS B.8(B)). Students should understand inheritance patterns that do not follow simple Mendelian rules. Key patterns: incomplete dominance (heterozygote shows blended phenotype, e.g., red x white = pink), codominance (both alleles fully expressed, e.g., AB blood type), multiple alleles (more than two alleles exist in the population, e.g., ABO blood types with IA, IB, i), sex-linked inheritance (genes on X chromosome, males more affected), polygenic inheritance (multiple genes influence one trait, e.g., skin color, height), epistasis (one gene masks expression of another). Key concepts: X-linked recessive conditions (color blindness, hemophilia) appear more frequently in males; pedigree analysis can trace inheritance patterns through families. Vocabulary: incomplete dominance, codominance, multiple alleles, sex-linked, autosomal, carrier, polygenic, epistasis, pedigree, X-linked. Common misconceptions: all traits follow simple dominant-recessive patterns; sex-linked disorders affect only males.

Biology.teks_description: Analyze how natural selection produces population change over time
Biology.prompt: This topic covers evolution by natural selection for Biology students (TEKS B.10(A)). Students should understand Darwin's theory and how populations change over time. Natural selection mechanism: variation exists in populations, some variations are heritable, organisms produce more offspring than can survive, individuals with advantageous traits are more likely to survive and reproduce (fitness). Key concepts: adaptation, speciation (allopatric and sympatric), evidence for evolution (fossil record, comparative anatomy, molecular biology, biogeography, embryology), types of selection (directional, stabilizing, disruptive). Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium describes conditions when evolution is NOT occurring (no mutation, random mating, no selection, large population, no migration). Vocabulary: natural selection, fitness, adaptation, speciation, homologous structures, analogous structures, vestigial structures, gene pool, allele frequency, genetic drift, gene flow, founder effect, bottleneck effect. Common misconceptions: evolution is goal-directed; individuals evolve (populations evolve); survival of the fittest means strongest.

Biology.teks_description: Analyze evidence of common ancestry through fossil records, biogeography, and homologies
Biology.prompt: This topic covers classification and taxonomy for Biology students (TEKS B.9(A)). Students should understand how organisms are classified based on evolutionary relationships. Linnaean hierarchy: Domain, Kingdom, Phylum, Class, Order, Family, Genus, Species. Three domains: Bacteria, Archaea, Eukarya. Six kingdoms: Bacteria, Archaea, Protista, Fungi, Plantae, Animalia. Key concepts: binomial nomenclature (Genus species), phylogenetic trees (cladograms) show evolutionary relationships, evidence for classification includes morphology, molecular data (DNA/RNA/protein sequences), fossils, biogeography, and embryological development. Cladistics groups organisms by shared derived characteristics (synapomorphies). Vocabulary: taxonomy, phylogeny, clade, common ancestor, monophyletic, outgroup, derived character, convergent evolution, divergent evolution, analogous structures, homologous structures. Students should be able to read and construct cladograms, identify common ancestors, and use evidence to justify classification decisions. Common misconceptions: classification is fixed and unchanging; more similar-looking organisms are always more closely related.

Biology.teks_description: Investigate ecological relationships including predation, parasitism, commensalism, mutualism, and competition
Biology.prompt: This topic covers ecology and ecosystems for Biology students (TEKS B.13(A)). Students should understand ecological relationships and how organisms interact within ecosystems. Levels of organization: organism, population, community, ecosystem, biome, biosphere. Ecological relationships: predation (predator-prey dynamics), parasitism (one benefits, one harmed), commensalism (one benefits, other unaffected), mutualism (both benefit), competition (intraspecific and interspecific). Key concepts: food chains and food webs, trophic levels (producers, primary consumers, secondary consumers, tertiary consumers, decomposers), energy transfer (10% rule), biomass pyramids. Vocabulary: niche, habitat, symbiosis, keystone species, trophic cascade, primary productivity, biomass, carrying capacity, limiting factor, competitive exclusion principle. Students should be able to trace energy flow through ecosystems, analyze food webs, and predict effects of removing species. Common misconceptions: ecosystems are static; decomposers are not important to energy flow.

Biology.teks_description: Explain how environmental changes affect biodiversity and ecosystem stability
Biology.prompt: This topic covers biomes and biodiversity for Biology students (TEKS B.13(D)). Students should understand how environmental changes affect biodiversity and ecosystem stability. Major terrestrial biomes: tropical rainforest (high biodiversity, warm and wet), desert (low precipitation, extreme temperatures), grassland/savanna (dominated by grasses, seasonal rainfall), temperate deciduous forest (four seasons, broad-leaf trees), taiga/boreal forest (coniferous, cold winters), tundra (permafrost, low biodiversity), chaparral (Mediterranean climate). Aquatic biomes: freshwater (lakes, rivers, wetlands) and marine (open ocean, coral reefs, estuaries, intertidal zones). Key concepts: climate determines biome distribution (temperature and precipitation), biodiversity varies by biome, human activities threaten biodiversity (habitat destruction, pollution, invasive species, climate change). Vocabulary: biome, biodiversity, climate, latitude, altitude, permafrost, canopy, understory, photic zone, aphotic zone, estuary, littoral zone. Common misconceptions: deserts are always hot; rainforests have the most fertile soil.

Biology.teks_description: Analyze disruptions to matter cycling and energy flow through trophic levels
Biology.prompt: This topic covers population dynamics for Biology students (TEKS B.13(B)). Students should understand how populations grow, are regulated, and how energy flows through trophic levels. Population growth models: exponential growth (J-curve, unlimited resources) vs. logistic growth (S-curve, limited resources approaching carrying capacity K). Growth rate equation: dN/dt = rN for exponential; dN/dt = rN((K-N)/K) for logistic. Key concepts: density-dependent limiting factors (competition, predation, disease, parasitism) vs. density-independent factors (natural disasters, climate, weather), carrying capacity, survivorship curves (Type I, II, III), age structure diagrams predict future population growth. Trophic levels and energy flow: only about 10% of energy transfers between trophic levels. Vocabulary: population density, dispersion, immigration, emigration, birth rate, death rate, carrying capacity, r-strategist, K-strategist, demographic transition, survivorship curve, trophic level. Common misconceptions: populations always grow exponentially; carrying capacity is fixed and unchanging.

Biology.teks_description: Explain carbon and nitrogen cycle significance and disruption consequences
Biology.prompt: This topic covers human impact on the environment for Biology students (TEKS B.13(C)). Students should understand biogeochemical cycles and how human activities disrupt them. Carbon cycle: carbon moves through atmosphere (CO2), organisms (photosynthesis/respiration), oceans, and fossil fuels; burning fossil fuels increases atmospheric CO2 causing enhanced greenhouse effect. Nitrogen cycle: nitrogen fixation (N2 to NH3 by bacteria), nitrification, denitrification; human impacts include fertilizer runoff causing eutrophication and algal blooms. Water cycle: evaporation, condensation, precipitation, transpiration, runoff; pollution affects water quality. Phosphorus cycle: rocks to soil to organisms; excess phosphorus from agriculture causes water pollution. Key concepts: greenhouse gases and global warming, deforestation effects, ozone depletion, acid rain, biomagnification, invasive species, habitat fragmentation, conservation strategies. Vocabulary: biogeochemical cycle, carbon sink, nitrogen fixation, eutrophication, biomagnification, greenhouse effect, deforestation, sustainability. Common misconceptions: the greenhouse effect is entirely bad (it is natural and necessary); ozone depletion causes global warming.

Biology.skill: Anatomy and Physiology of Major Systems
Biology.teks_description: Analyze animal system interactions for regulation, nutrient absorption, reproduction, and defense
Biology.prompt: This topic covers anatomy and physiology of major body systems for Biology students (TEKS B.12(A)). Students should understand how animal body systems interact to maintain homeostasis. Key systems: circulatory (heart, blood vessels, blood; transports O2, nutrients, waste), respiratory (lungs, gas exchange; O2 in, CO2 out), digestive (mechanical and chemical breakdown; mouth, stomach, intestines), nervous (brain, spinal cord, neurons; electrochemical signals), endocrine (hormones, glands; chemical regulation), excretory/urinary (kidneys, nephrons; waste removal, water balance), musculoskeletal (bones, muscles; support and movement), reproductive (gamete production, fertilization), immune (defense against pathogens). Key concepts: feedback loops (negative feedback maintains homeostasis, positive feedback amplifies response), systems work together for regulation, nutrient absorption, reproduction, and defense. Vocabulary: homeostasis, organ system, tissue, feedback loop, hormone, neuron, synapse, antibody, antigen. Common misconceptions: each body system works independently; the heart pumps only oxygenated blood.

Biology.teks_description: Compare viral versus cellular structures and explain disease transmission
Biology.prompt: This topic covers viruses and bacteria for Biology students (TEKS B.5(D)). Students should understand the structural differences between viruses and cells, and how diseases are transmitted. Viruses: not considered living, composed of nucleic acid (DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protein coat (capsid), some have lipid envelopes; reproduce only inside host cells (obligate intracellular parasites). Viral reproduction: lytic cycle (immediate replication and cell lysis) and lysogenic cycle (viral DNA integrates into host genome). Bacteria: prokaryotic cells, circular DNA, no membrane-bound organelles, cell wall (peptidoglycan in most), reproduce by binary fission. Shapes: coccus (sphere), bacillus (rod), spirillum (spiral). Key concepts: antibiotics target bacteria not viruses, antibiotic resistance through natural selection, beneficial bacteria (gut microbiome, decomposition, nitrogen fixation), disease transmission (direct contact, airborne, vector-borne, waterborne). Vocabulary: pathogen, capsid, bacteriophage, lytic, lysogenic, binary fission, antibiotic resistance, vaccine, epidemic, pandemic. Common misconceptions: antibiotics work on viruses; all bacteria are harmful.

Biology.teks_description: Analyze animal system interactions including defense and immune response mechanisms
Biology.prompt: This topic covers the immune system for Biology students (TEKS B.12(A)). Students should understand how the body defends against pathogens through multiple defense mechanisms. First line of defense: physical and chemical barriers (skin, mucous membranes, stomach acid, tears, saliva). Second line of defense: nonspecific innate immune response (inflammation, fever, phagocytes like macrophages and neutrophils, natural killer cells, complement proteins). Third line of defense: adaptive (specific) immune response involving lymphocytes. B cells produce antibodies (humoral immunity); T cells attack infected cells directly (cell-mediated immunity). Key concepts: antigen recognition, antibody specificity (lock-and-key), memory cells provide long-term immunity, active immunity (vaccination or infection) vs. passive immunity (antibodies transferred), autoimmune diseases (immune system attacks self), allergies (overreaction to harmless substances), immunodeficiency. Vocabulary: antigen, antibody, lymphocyte, B cell, T cell (helper, cytotoxic, memory), phagocyte, inflammation, vaccine, pathogen, immune response. Common misconceptions: vaccines cause the disease; the immune system is one single organ.

Biology.teks_description: Explain plant system interactions involving transport, reproduction, and response to environment
Biology.prompt: This topic covers plant structure and function for Biology students (TEKS B.12(B)). Students should understand how plant systems interact for transport, reproduction, and environmental response. Plant tissue types: dermal (protection), vascular (transport), ground (photosynthesis, storage, support). Vascular tissue: xylem (transports water and minerals upward, driven by transpiration pull and cohesion-tension) and phloem (transports sugars bidirectionally via translocation and pressure flow). Plant organs: roots (absorption, anchorage), stems (support, transport), leaves (photosynthesis, gas exchange through stomata). Reproduction: alternation of generations (sporophyte and gametophyte), flower structure (sepals, petals, stamens, pistil), pollination, fertilization, seed and fruit development. Plant responses: tropisms (phototropism, gravitropism, thigmotropism), hormones (auxin, gibberellin, cytokinin, ethylene, abscisic acid), photoperiodism. Vocabulary: transpiration, translocation, meristem, cambium, stomata, guard cell, xylem, phloem, germination, tropism, hormone. Common misconceptions: plants get food from soil; water moves up through plants by capillary action alone.

Biology.teks_description: Discuss PCR, gel electrophoresis, and genetic engineering applications
Biology.prompt: This topic covers biotechnology for Biology students (TEKS B.7(D)). Students should understand modern biotechnology tools and their applications. PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction): amplifies specific DNA segments using primers, DNA polymerase, and thermal cycling (denature at 95C, anneal primers at 55C, extend at 72C); produces millions of copies. Gel electrophoresis: separates DNA fragments by size using an electric field through agarose gel; smaller fragments move farther; used for DNA fingerprinting, forensics, and paternity testing. Genetic engineering: using restriction enzymes to cut DNA at specific recognition sequences, ligase to join DNA fragments, plasmids as vectors to insert genes into organisms, recombinant DNA technology. Applications: gene therapy, transgenic organisms (GMOs), pharmaceutical production (insulin, growth hormone), forensic identification, agricultural improvements. Vocabulary: PCR, primer, thermal cycler, gel electrophoresis, restriction enzyme, ligase, plasmid, vector, recombinant DNA, transgenic, clone, GMO, CRISPR. Common misconceptions: GMOs are inherently dangerous; cloning produces identical organisms in every way.

Biology.teks_description: Discuss PCR, gel electrophoresis, and genetic engineering applications and implications
Biology.prompt: This topic covers genetic engineering for Biology students (TEKS B.7(D)). Students should understand advanced genetic engineering techniques, applications, and ethical implications. Key techniques: recombinant DNA technology (combining DNA from different sources), gene cloning (inserting genes into vectors like plasmids for replication in host organisms), CRISPR-Cas9 (precise gene editing using guide RNA and Cas9 enzyme to cut DNA at specific locations). Applications: medical (gene therapy for genetic disorders, production of human insulin in bacteria, development of vaccines), agricultural (herbicide-resistant crops, Bt crops producing insect-resistant proteins, golden rice with enhanced vitamin A), industrial (biofuels, bioremediation, enzyme production). Ethical considerations: safety of GMO consumption, environmental concerns (gene flow to wild populations, biodiversity loss), designer babies debate, intellectual property of genetically modified organisms, equitable access to genetic technologies. Vocabulary: CRISPR, Cas9, guide RNA, gene therapy, transgenic organism, knockout gene, gene drive, bioethics, informed consent, bioremediation. Students should evaluate both benefits and risks of genetic engineering technologies.

Chemistry.skill: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table
Chemistry.teks_description: Explain the Periodic Table's development over time using chemical and physical properties evidence
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers atomic structure and the Periodic Table for Chemistry students (TEKS C.5(A)). Students should understand how the periodic table was developed using patterns in chemical and physical properties. Atomic structure: protons (positive, in nucleus, determine element identity), neutrons (neutral, in nucleus, determine isotope), electrons (negative, in electron cloud, determine chemical behavior). Electron configuration: Aufbau principle, Pauli exclusion principle, Hund's rule; notation using orbital diagrams and electron configurations (1s2 2s2 2p6...). Periodic trends: atomic radius (decreases across period, increases down group), ionization energy (increases across, decreases down), electronegativity (increases across, decreases down), electron affinity. Groups: alkali metals (1), alkaline earth metals (2), halogens (17), noble gases (18), transition metals. Key concepts: valence electrons determine chemical properties, metals vs. nonmetals vs. metalloids, Mendeleev's predictions. Vocabulary: atomic number, mass number, isotope, ion, orbital, sublevel, period, group, valence electron, core electron. Common misconceptions: electrons orbit like planets; the periodic table is just for memorization.

Chemistry.teks_description: Construct arguments supporting periodic trend predictions of bonding between elements
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers chemical bonding for Chemistry students (TEKS C.7(A)). Students should understand how atoms bond based on periodic trends and electron configurations. Types of bonds: ionic (transfer of electrons between metals and nonmetals, held by electrostatic attraction), covalent (sharing of electrons between nonmetals, can be single, double, or triple bonds), metallic (sea of delocalized electrons among metal atoms). Key concepts: octet rule (atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve 8 valence electrons), Lewis dot structures show valence electrons and bonding, electronegativity differences determine bond type (nonpolar covalent <0.5, polar covalent 0.5-1.7, ionic >1.7). Bond properties: bond length, bond energy, polarity. Intermolecular forces: London dispersion (all molecules), dipole-dipole (polar molecules), hydrogen bonding (H bonded to F, O, or N). Vocabulary: ionic bond, covalent bond, metallic bond, electronegativity, polarity, Lewis structure, lone pair, bonding pair, lattice energy, crystal lattice. Common misconceptions: ionic compounds form molecules; all covalent bonds share electrons equally.

Chemistry.teks_description: Name and write chemical formulas for ionic and covalent compounds using IUPAC nomenclature rules
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers nomenclature and chemical formulas for Chemistry students (TEKS C.7(B)). Students should master IUPAC naming rules for ionic and covalent compounds. Ionic compounds: name the cation (metal) first, then anion (nonmetal with -ide suffix); for transition metals, use Roman numerals to indicate charge (e.g., Iron(III) chloride = FeCl3). Polyatomic ions: memorize common ions (sulfate SO4^2-, nitrate NO3^-, phosphate PO4^3-, carbonate CO3^2-, hydroxide OH^-, ammonium NH4^+). Covalent (molecular) compounds: use Greek prefixes (mono-, di-, tri-, tetra-, penta-, hexa-) for both elements, second element gets -ide suffix (e.g., dinitrogen pentoxide = N2O5). Writing formulas: use charge-crossing method for ionic compounds, ensure formulas are reduced to lowest whole-number ratios. Acids: binary acids (hydro- prefix, -ic suffix: HCl = hydrochloric acid), oxyacids (-ate polyatomic ion becomes -ic acid, -ite becomes -ous acid). Vocabulary: cation, anion, polyatomic ion, binary compound, formula unit, molecular formula, empirical formula, subscript, coefficient. Common misconceptions: all compounds with metals are ionic; the order of elements in a formula does not matter.

Chemistry.teks_description: Interpret, write, and balance equations including synthesis, decomposition, single/double replacement, and combustion
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers chemical reactions and equations for Chemistry students (TEKS C.9(A)). Students should be able to interpret, write, and balance chemical equations. Five main reaction types: synthesis/combination (A + B → AB), decomposition (AB → A + B), single replacement (A + BC → AC + B, using activity series), double replacement (AB + CD → AD + CB, driven by precipitate, gas, or water formation), combustion (hydrocarbon + O2 → CO2 + H2O). Balancing equations: apply law of conservation of mass (atoms in = atoms out), adjust coefficients (never subscripts), balance metals first, then nonmetals, then hydrogen and oxygen last. Key concepts: identifying reaction types from equations, predicting products, using the activity series for single replacement reactions, solubility rules for double replacement. Vocabulary: reactant, product, coefficient, subscript, yield, catalyst, activity series, precipitate, spectator ion, net ionic equation, combustion, exothermic, endothermic. Common misconceptions: chemical equations show individual molecules reacting; changing subscripts is acceptable when balancing.

Chemistry.teks_description: Perform stoichiometric calculations for mass relationships, gas volumes, and percent yield
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers stoichiometry for Chemistry students (TEKS C.9(C)). Students should perform calculations relating masses, moles, and volumes in chemical reactions. Core concept: mole ratios from balanced equations allow conversion between amounts of reactants and products. Problem-solving steps: (1) write and balance the equation, (2) convert given quantity to moles, (3) use mole ratio to find moles of desired substance, (4) convert to desired units. Key calculations: mole-to-mole conversions, mass-to-mass conversions, limiting reagent problems (the reactant that runs out first determines theoretical yield), percent yield = (actual yield / theoretical yield) x 100%. Gas stoichiometry: at STP, 1 mol of any gas occupies 22.4 L. Molar mass connects grams to moles. Avogadro's number (6.022 x 10^23) connects moles to particles. Vocabulary: stoichiometry, mole ratio, limiting reagent, excess reagent, theoretical yield, actual yield, percent yield, molar mass, STP, Avogadro's number. Common misconceptions: the larger amount of reactant is always the limiting reagent; coefficients represent grams.

Chemistry.skill: States of Matter and Phase Changes
Chemistry.teks_description: Describe kinetic molecular theory postulates relating to states of matter
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers states of matter and phase changes for Chemistry students (TEKS C.10(A)). Students should understand the kinetic molecular theory (KMT) and how it explains the behavior of matter in different states. KMT postulates: particles are in constant random motion, no intermolecular forces in ideal gases, kinetic energy is proportional to temperature, elastic collisions. States of matter: solid (fixed shape and volume, particles vibrate in place), liquid (fixed volume, variable shape, particles slide past each other), gas (variable shape and volume, particles move freely). Phase changes: melting (solid→liquid), freezing (liquid→solid), vaporization/boiling (liquid→gas), condensation (gas→liquid), sublimation (solid→gas), deposition (gas→solid). Key concepts: heating curves show temperature vs. energy added, plateaus during phase changes (energy changes potential energy not kinetic), heat of fusion and heat of vaporization. Vocabulary: kinetic energy, temperature, pressure, phase diagram, triple point, critical point, vapor pressure, boiling point, melting point, heat of fusion, heat of vaporization. Common misconceptions: boiling point is always 100C (it depends on pressure); bubbles in boiling water are hydrogen and oxygen.

Chemistry.teks_description: Investigate how solid and gas solubilities are influenced by temperature using solubility curves
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers solutions and solubility for Chemistry students (TEKS C.11(C)). Students should understand how solutions form and factors affecting solubility. Solution components: solute (dissolved substance) and solvent (dissolving medium, usually water). Types of solutions: unsaturated (can dissolve more), saturated (maximum solute dissolved at given temperature), supersaturated (contains more than normally possible, unstable). Factors affecting solubility: temperature (most solids become more soluble as temperature increases; gases become less soluble), pressure (affects gas solubility per Henry's law), nature of solute and solvent (like dissolves like: polar dissolves polar, nonpolar dissolves nonpolar). Solubility curves: graphs showing grams of solute per 100g water vs. temperature; use to determine saturation status and amount that crystallizes upon cooling. Concentration units: molarity (M = mol solute / L solution), percent composition, parts per million (ppm). Vocabulary: solute, solvent, solubility, saturated, unsaturated, supersaturated, miscible, immiscible, dilution, concentration, molarity. Common misconceptions: all substances become more soluble at higher temperatures; dissolving is a chemical change.

Chemistry.teks_description: Define pH and calculate solution pH using hydrogen ion concentration
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers acids, bases, and pH for Chemistry students (TEKS C.12(E)). Students should understand acid-base chemistry and pH calculations. Definitions: Arrhenius (acids produce H+ in water, bases produce OH-), Bronsted-Lowry (acids donate protons, bases accept protons). pH scale: ranges from 0-14; pH = -log[H+]; pH < 7 is acidic, pH = 7 is neutral, pH > 7 is basic. pOH = -log[OH-]; pH + pOH = 14. Key concepts: strong acids completely dissociate (HCl, H2SO4, HNO3), weak acids partially dissociate (CH3COOH); strong bases completely dissociate (NaOH, KOH), weak bases partially dissociate (NH3). Neutralization: acid + base → salt + water. Each pH unit represents a tenfold change in H+ concentration. Indicators change color at specific pH ranges. Buffer solutions resist pH changes. Vocabulary: acid, base, conjugate acid, conjugate base, amphoteric, neutralization, titration, equivalence point, indicator, buffer, hydronium ion, hydroxide ion, dissociation. Common misconceptions: pH 0 means no acid; all acids are dangerous; neutral pH always equals 7 (only in pure water at 25C).

Chemistry.teks_description: Perform calculations involving heat, mass, temperature change, and specific heat
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers thermochemistry for Chemistry students (TEKS C.13(D)). Students should understand energy changes in chemical reactions and perform calorimetry calculations. Key equation: q = mcΔT where q = heat energy (joules), m = mass (grams), c = specific heat capacity (J/g·°C), ΔT = temperature change (Tf - Ti). Specific heat of water: 4.184 J/g·°C. Exothermic reactions release heat (ΔH negative, temperature of surroundings increases); endothermic reactions absorb heat (ΔH positive, temperature of surroundings decreases). Key concepts: enthalpy (H) is heat content at constant pressure, Hess's law (enthalpy changes are additive for stepwise reactions), standard enthalpy of formation, calorimetry measures heat transfer. Energy diagrams show activation energy, reactant and product energy levels, and effect of catalysts (lower activation energy). Vocabulary: enthalpy, exothermic, endothermic, calorimeter, specific heat, heat capacity, Hess's law, activation energy, enthalpy of formation, thermochemical equation, joule, calorie. Common misconceptions: exothermic means explosive; temperature and heat are the same thing.

Chemistry.teks_description: Differentiate among acid-base, precipitation, and oxidation-reduction reactions
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers reaction rates and chemical equilibrium for Chemistry students (TEKS C.9(B)). Students should understand factors affecting reaction rates and equilibrium concepts. Reaction rates: measure how fast reactants are consumed or products formed. Factors affecting rate: concentration (higher = faster), temperature (higher = faster, more kinetic energy), surface area (greater = faster), catalysts (lower activation energy), nature of reactants. Collision theory: reactions occur when particles collide with sufficient energy (activation energy) and proper orientation. Chemical equilibrium: dynamic state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal; concentrations remain constant but reactions continue. Le Chatelier's principle: when a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to counteract the change (adding reactant shifts right, removing product shifts right, increasing temperature shifts toward endothermic direction, pressure changes affect gas-phase equilibria). Equilibrium constant Keq = [products]/[reactants]. Vocabulary: reaction rate, collision theory, activation energy, catalyst, equilibrium, reversible reaction, Le Chatelier's principle, equilibrium constant, shift. Common misconceptions: equilibrium means equal concentrations of reactants and products; catalysts shift equilibrium.

Chemistry.teks_description: Differentiate among acid-base, precipitation, and oxidation-reduction reactions
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers oxidation-reduction (redox) reactions for Chemistry students (TEKS C.9(B)). Students should understand electron transfer in chemical reactions. Key definitions: oxidation is loss of electrons (OIL), reduction is gain of electrons (RIG); together: OIL RIG. Oxidation numbers (states): rules for assigning (free elements = 0, monatomic ions = charge, O usually -2, H usually +1, sum of oxidation numbers = charge of species). Identifying redox: if oxidation numbers change, it is a redox reaction. Reducing agent: substance that is oxidized (gives electrons); oxidizing agent: substance that is reduced (accepts electrons). Types of redox reactions: combustion, single replacement, synthesis of metal oxides, electrochemical cells. Activity series: ranks metals by reactivity; more active metals can displace less active metals from solutions. Vocabulary: oxidation, reduction, oxidizing agent, reducing agent, oxidation number, half-reaction, electron transfer, activity series, redox, electrochemical cell. Common misconceptions: oxidation always involves oxygen; reduction means something gets smaller.

Chemistry.teks_description: Explain the Periodic Table including carbon family properties and organic chemistry foundations
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers organic chemistry basics for Chemistry students (TEKS C.5(A)). Students should understand carbon's unique bonding properties and basic organic compounds. Carbon: has 4 valence electrons, can form 4 covalent bonds, can bond to itself creating chains, rings, and branched structures; this versatility explains the enormous diversity of organic compounds. Hydrocarbons: compounds of only carbon and hydrogen. Alkanes (single bonds, CnH2n+2, saturated), alkenes (at least one double bond, CnH2n, unsaturated), alkynes (at least one triple bond, CnH2n-2, unsaturated), aromatic (benzene ring, C6H6). Isomers: same molecular formula, different structural arrangement (structural isomers). Key properties: nonpolar (insoluble in water, soluble in organic solvents), boiling points increase with molecular size, London dispersion forces are primary intermolecular forces. Naming: IUPAC system uses root name for longest carbon chain (meth-, eth-, prop-, but-, pent-), suffix indicates bond type (-ane, -ene, -yne). Vocabulary: organic, hydrocarbon, saturated, unsaturated, isomer, functional group, polymer, monomer, IUPAC nomenclature. Common misconceptions: organic means natural or healthy; only living things produce organic compounds.

Chemistry.teks_description: Analyze ionic, covalent, and metallic substance properties regarding intramolecular and intermolecular forces
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers hydrocarbons and functional groups for Chemistry students (TEKS C.7(D)). Students should understand how functional groups determine the properties of organic molecules. Major functional groups: hydroxyl (-OH, alcohols: polar, hydrogen bonding, higher boiling points), carbonyl (C=O, aldehydes and ketones), carboxyl (-COOH, carboxylic acids: weak acids, found in vinegar and amino acids), amino (-NH2, amines: basic, found in amino acids), ester (-COO-, formed from acid + alcohol, fragrances and flavors), ether (-O-, relatively unreactive), phosphate (-PO4, found in DNA and ATP). Intramolecular forces: covalent bonds within molecules determine structure. Intermolecular forces: determine physical properties; hydrogen bonding (alcohols, carboxylic acids), dipole-dipole (polar groups), London dispersion (all molecules, stronger with larger size). Key concepts: functional groups determine reactivity and physical properties, polymers are large molecules formed from repeating monomers (addition and condensation polymerization). Vocabulary: functional group, alcohol, aldehyde, ketone, carboxylic acid, ester, amine, ether, polymer, monomer, intermolecular force, intramolecular force. Common misconceptions: all organic molecules have the same properties; functional groups do not affect physical properties.

Chemistry.teks_description: Describe alpha, beta, and gamma radioactive decay characteristics using balanced nuclear equations
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers nuclear chemistry for Chemistry students (TEKS C.14(A)). Students should understand types of radioactive decay and nuclear reactions. Types of decay: alpha decay (emits helium nucleus, mass decreases by 4, atomic number by 2), beta decay (neutron converts to proton, emits electron, atomic number increases by 1), gamma decay (emits high-energy photon, no change in mass or atomic number). Balancing nuclear equations: conserve mass number (top) and atomic number (bottom). Half-life: time for half of a radioactive sample to decay; calculate remaining amount using N = N0 × (1/2)^(t/t½). Nuclear fission: heavy nucleus splits into smaller nuclei, releases large energy (nuclear power plants, atomic bombs). Nuclear fusion: light nuclei combine to form heavier nucleus, releases even more energy (stars, sun). Key concepts: band of stability, nuclear binding energy, E = mc², applications (medical imaging, cancer treatment, carbon dating, nuclear power, smoke detectors). Vocabulary: radioactive decay, isotope, radioisotope, half-life, fission, fusion, chain reaction, critical mass, transmutation, nuclear equation. Common misconceptions: all radiation is harmful; nuclear reactions and chemical reactions are the same process.

Chemistry.teks_description: Describe and calculate relationships among volume, pressure, moles, and temperature for ideal gases
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers gas laws for Chemistry students (TEKS C.10(B)). Students should understand and apply mathematical relationships among pressure, volume, temperature, and moles of gases. Individual gas laws: Boyle's law (P1V1 = P2V2, inverse relationship at constant T and n), Charles's law (V1/T1 = V2/T2, direct relationship at constant P and n), Gay-Lussac's law (P1/T1 = P2/T2, direct at constant V and n), Avogadro's law (V1/n1 = V2/n2, direct at constant T and P). Combined gas law: P1V1/T1 = P2V2/T2. Ideal gas law: PV = nRT where R = 0.0821 L·atm/mol·K or 8.314 J/mol·K. Dalton's law of partial pressures: Ptotal = P1 + P2 + P3... Temperature must be in Kelvin (K = °C + 273). STP: 0°C (273 K) and 1 atm; molar volume = 22.4 L/mol. Key concepts: ideal vs. real gases (real gases deviate at high pressure and low temperature). Vocabulary: pressure, volume, temperature, moles, STP, ideal gas, real gas, partial pressure, molar volume, Kelvin, atmosphere, torr, pascal. Common misconceptions: gas particles are stationary; temperature can be used in Celsius for gas law calculations.

Chemistry.teks_description: Define mole and apply molar mass concepts to convert between moles and grams
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers the mole concept for Chemistry students (TEKS C.8(A)). Students should understand the mole as the fundamental counting unit in chemistry and perform conversions between moles, grams, and particles. One mole = 6.022 × 10^23 particles (Avogadro's number), equivalent to the number of atoms in exactly 12 grams of carbon-12. Molar mass: the mass of one mole of a substance in grams, numerically equal to the atomic/molecular mass in amu from the periodic table. Key conversions: grams to moles (divide by molar mass), moles to grams (multiply by molar mass), moles to particles (multiply by Avogadro's number), particles to moles (divide by Avogadro's number). Molar mass of compounds: sum of all atomic masses (e.g., H2O = 2(1.008) + 16.00 = 18.016 g/mol). Percent composition: (mass of element in 1 mol compound / molar mass of compound) × 100%. Empirical formula: simplest whole-number ratio of atoms; molecular formula: actual number of atoms. Vocabulary: mole, molar mass, Avogadro's number, formula mass, empirical formula, molecular formula, percent composition. Common misconceptions: a mole is a mass (it is a count); molar mass and atomic mass are the same thing.

Chemistry.teks_description: Interpret, write, and balance equations including synthesis, decomposition, single replacement, double replacement, and combustion
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers types of chemical reactions for Chemistry students (TEKS C.9(A)). Students should identify, classify, and predict products for the five main reaction types. Synthesis (combination): two or more substances combine to form one product (A + B → AB; e.g., 2Na + Cl2 → 2NaCl). Decomposition: one compound breaks into two or more simpler substances (AB → A + B; e.g., 2H2O → 2H2 + O2). Single replacement: one element replaces another in a compound (A + BC → AC + B; use activity series to predict if reaction occurs; e.g., Zn + CuSO4 → ZnSO4 + Cu). Double replacement: ions in two compounds exchange partners (AB + CD → AD + CB; driven by formation of precipitate, gas, or water; e.g., NaCl + AgNO3 → AgCl + NaNO3). Combustion: substance reacts with O2, producing CO2 and H2O for hydrocarbons (CxHy + O2 → CO2 + H2O). Key concepts: conservation of mass, balancing equations, predicting products, net ionic equations. Vocabulary: synthesis, decomposition, single replacement, double replacement, combustion, activity series, precipitate, spectator ion. Common misconceptions: all single replacement reactions occur spontaneously; all reactions fit neatly into five categories.

Chemistry.teks_description: Describe water's unique role in solutions regarding polarity
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers the unique properties of water for Chemistry students (TEKS C.11(A)). Students should understand how water's molecular structure leads to its exceptional properties. Water molecule: bent shape (104.5° bond angle), polar covalent O-H bonds, oxygen is more electronegative creating partial charges (δ- on O, δ+ on H). Hydrogen bonding: strong intermolecular force between water molecules; each water molecule can form up to 4 hydrogen bonds. Properties resulting from polarity and hydrogen bonding: high specific heat capacity (4.184 J/g·°C, resists temperature change, moderates climate), high heat of vaporization (cooling effect of evaporation), surface tension (cohesion creates water droplets, insects walking on water), capillary action (adhesion and cohesion allow water to climb narrow tubes), universal solvent (dissolves ionic and polar substances), ice is less dense than liquid water (hydrogen bonds form crystal lattice, ice floats, insulates aquatic ecosystems). Vocabulary: polarity, hydrogen bond, cohesion, adhesion, surface tension, capillary action, specific heat, heat of vaporization, universal solvent, hydrophobic, hydrophilic. Common misconceptions: water is the only polar molecule; ice sinks in water.

Chemistry.teks_description: Classify processes as exothermic or endothermic and represent energy changes using thermochemical equations
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers chemical kinetics and energy changes for Chemistry students (TEKS C.13(C)). Students should classify reactions by energy changes and understand reaction energetics. Exothermic processes: release energy to surroundings, ΔH < 0, products have lower energy than reactants (e.g., combustion, neutralization, most crystallization). Endothermic processes: absorb energy from surroundings, ΔH > 0, products have higher energy than reactants (e.g., photosynthesis, melting ice, dissolving ammonium nitrate). Thermochemical equations: balanced equations including enthalpy change (ΔH value). Energy diagrams: plot potential energy vs. reaction progress, showing reactant energy, product energy, activation energy (Ea), and transition state. Catalysts lower activation energy without being consumed; they do not change ΔH. Key concepts: system vs. surroundings, law of conservation of energy (first law of thermodynamics), enthalpy vs. entropy, spontaneity (Gibbs free energy ΔG = ΔH - TΔS). Vocabulary: exothermic, endothermic, enthalpy, entropy, activation energy, catalyst, transition state, thermochemical equation, Gibbs free energy, spontaneous, nonspontaneous. Common misconceptions: endothermic reactions cannot be spontaneous; catalysts provide energy for the reaction.

Chemistry.teks_description: Differentiate among acid-base, precipitation, and oxidation-reduction reactions including electrochemical processes
Chemistry.prompt: This topic covers electrochemistry for Chemistry students (TEKS C.9(B)). Students should understand the relationship between chemical reactions and electrical energy. Electrochemistry links redox reactions to electricity. Galvanic (voltaic) cells: spontaneous redox reactions produce electrical energy; anode (oxidation occurs, negative terminal), cathode (reduction occurs, positive terminal), salt bridge maintains electrical neutrality, electron flow from anode to cathode through external circuit. Electrolytic cells: nonspontaneous reactions driven by external electrical energy (electrolysis); used for electroplating, purifying metals, decomposing compounds. Standard reduction potentials (E°): measure tendency to gain electrons; more positive = stronger oxidizing agent; cell potential E°cell = E°cathode - E°anode; positive E°cell means spontaneous. Key concepts: half-reactions, balancing redox equations, Faraday's laws of electrolysis, applications (batteries, fuel cells, corrosion prevention, electroplating). Vocabulary: galvanic cell, electrolytic cell, anode, cathode, salt bridge, half-reaction, cell potential, standard reduction potential, electrolysis, electroplating, corrosion. Common misconceptions: electrons flow through the salt bridge; electrolytic and galvanic cells work the same way.

Physics.skill: Kinematics (Motion in One and Two Dimensions)
Physics.teks_description: Describe one-dimensional motion using distance, displacement, speed, velocity, frames of reference, and acceleration
Physics.prompt: This topic covers kinematics for Physics students (TEKS Phy.5(C)). Students should describe and analyze motion in one and two dimensions using mathematical relationships. One-dimensional kinematics equations: v = v0 + at, x = x0 + v0t + ½at², v² = v0² + 2a(x - x0), x = ½(v + v0)t. Key concepts: distinguish distance (scalar) from displacement (vector), speed (scalar) from velocity (vector), average vs. instantaneous values. Motion graphs: position-time (slope = velocity), velocity-time (slope = acceleration, area = displacement), acceleration-time graphs. Two-dimensional motion: projectile motion combines constant horizontal velocity with vertical acceleration due to gravity (g = 9.8 m/s²); horizontal and vertical components are independent. Free fall: all objects accelerate at g regardless of mass (ignoring air resistance). Frames of reference: motion is relative to the observer. Vocabulary: displacement, velocity, acceleration, scalar, vector, projectile, trajectory, free fall, frame of reference, component. Common misconceptions: heavier objects fall faster; an object at the highest point of its trajectory has zero acceleration.

Physics.skill: Forces and Newton's Laws of Motion
Physics.teks_description: Calculate force effects using free body diagrams and Newton's second law
Physics.prompt: This topic covers forces and Newton's laws for Physics students (TEKS Phy.5(F)). Students should analyze forces using free body diagrams and apply Newton's laws. Newton's First Law (Inertia): an object at rest stays at rest and an object in motion stays in motion unless acted upon by a net force. Newton's Second Law: F_net = ma; acceleration is directly proportional to net force and inversely proportional to mass. Newton's Third Law: for every action force, there is an equal and opposite reaction force. Free body diagrams: show all forces acting on an object (gravity, normal force, tension, friction, applied force). Friction: static friction (prevents motion, fs ≤ μsN) and kinetic friction (opposes motion, fk = μkN). Weight vs. mass: weight = mg (force, measured in Newtons), mass is amount of matter (kg). Key concepts: net force, equilibrium (Fnet = 0), inclined planes (resolve forces into parallel and perpendicular components), connected objects, Atwood machines. Vocabulary: inertia, net force, normal force, tension, friction coefficient, equilibrium, free body diagram, newton, acceleration. Common misconceptions: heavier objects need more force to move at constant speed on frictionless surfaces; force is needed to maintain motion.

Physics.teks_description: Calculate and explain work and power; identify when work occurs
Physics.prompt: This topic covers work, energy, and power for Physics students (TEKS Phy.7(A)). Students should calculate work and power and understand energy transformations. Work: W = Fd cos(θ), where F is force, d is displacement, and θ is the angle between force and displacement direction; measured in joules (J). Work is done only when force causes displacement. Power: P = W/t = Fv, the rate of doing work; measured in watts (W). Types of energy: kinetic energy KE = ½mv², gravitational potential energy PE = mgh, elastic potential energy PE = ½kx². Conservation of energy: total mechanical energy (KE + PE) is conserved in the absence of non-conservative forces. Work-energy theorem: net work equals change in kinetic energy (Wnet = ΔKE). Key concepts: conservative forces (gravity, springs) vs. non-conservative forces (friction, air resistance), energy transformations in real systems (some energy converts to thermal energy via friction). Vocabulary: work, energy, power, kinetic energy, potential energy, mechanical energy, conservation of energy, watt, joule, conservative force. Common misconceptions: holding a heavy object requires work; energy is used up rather than transformed.

Physics.teks_description: Calculate impulse and momentum in physical systems
Physics.prompt: This topic covers momentum and impulse for Physics students (TEKS Phy.7(D)). Students should calculate and apply momentum and impulse in physical systems. Momentum: p = mv (mass × velocity), a vector quantity measured in kg·m/s. Impulse: J = FΔt = Δp (force × time interval = change in momentum). Impulse-momentum theorem: the impulse on an object equals its change in momentum. Conservation of momentum: in an isolated system (no external forces), total momentum before = total momentum after. Types of collisions: elastic (both momentum and kinetic energy conserved, objects bounce apart), inelastic (momentum conserved but kinetic energy not, objects may deform), perfectly inelastic (objects stick together, maximum KE loss). Key equations: m1v1i + m2v2i = m1v1f + m2v2f (general), m1v1i + m2v2i = (m1 + m2)vf (perfectly inelastic). Applications: car safety (airbags increase time to reduce force), sports (follow-through increases impulse), rocket propulsion. Vocabulary: momentum, impulse, conservation of momentum, elastic collision, inelastic collision, isolated system, recoil. Common misconceptions: heavier objects always have more momentum; momentum is the same as force.

Physics.teks_description: Describe and analyze uniform circular and projectile motion in two dimensions
Physics.prompt: This topic covers circular motion and rotation for Physics students (TEKS Phy.5(D)). Students should analyze uniform circular motion and projectile motion in two dimensions. Uniform circular motion: object moves in a circle at constant speed but changing velocity (direction changes). Centripetal acceleration: ac = v²/r, always directed toward the center of the circle. Centripetal force: Fc = mv²/r, the net force causing circular motion (not a separate force but provided by tension, gravity, friction, or normal force). Period: T = 2πr/v, time for one complete revolution. Frequency: f = 1/T, revolutions per unit time. Projectile motion: combination of horizontal (constant velocity) and vertical (constant acceleration g = 9.8 m/s²) motion; trajectory is parabolic. Range, maximum height, and time of flight depend on launch angle and initial velocity. Key concepts: centripetal vs. centrifugal (centrifugal is a fictitious force in rotating reference frames), banking of curves, satellites in orbit. Vocabulary: centripetal, centrifugal, period, frequency, angular velocity, tangential velocity, radians, projectile, trajectory, range. Common misconceptions: centrifugal force pushes objects outward; objects in circular motion are in equilibrium.

Physics.teks_description: Calculate gravitational force magnitude using Newton's law of universal gravitation
Physics.prompt: This topic covers gravitation for Physics students (TEKS Phy.5(H)). Students should understand and apply Newton's law of universal gravitation. Newton's law of universal gravitation: F = Gm1m2/r², where G = 6.674 × 10^-11 N·m²/kg² is the gravitational constant, m1 and m2 are the masses of two objects, and r is the distance between their centers. Key concepts: gravitational force is always attractive, acts between all masses, and follows an inverse-square relationship with distance. Gravitational field strength: g = GM/r², on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s². Orbital motion: satellites maintain orbit when gravitational force provides centripetal force (Gm1m2/r² = mv²/r). Kepler's laws: (1) planets orbit in ellipses with the sun at one focus, (2) equal areas in equal times, (3) T² ∝ r³. Weight varies by location (depends on local g), mass is constant. Escape velocity: minimum speed to escape gravitational field. Vocabulary: gravitational force, gravitational constant, gravitational field, orbit, satellite, weight, mass, inverse-square law, Kepler's laws, escape velocity. Common misconceptions: gravity only exists on Earth; astronauts are weightless because there is no gravity in space (they are in free fall).

Physics.teks_description: Examine simple harmonic motion and wave energy propagation in various media
Physics.prompt: This topic covers simple harmonic motion (SHM) for Physics students (TEKS Phy.8(A)). Students should understand oscillatory motion and wave energy propagation. SHM occurs when a restoring force is proportional to displacement from equilibrium (F = -kx for springs). Examples: mass on a spring, simple pendulum (for small angles). Key equations: period of spring T = 2π√(m/k), period of pendulum T = 2π√(L/g). Energy in SHM: continuous exchange between kinetic and potential energy; total mechanical energy is conserved. At equilibrium: maximum KE, zero PE; at maximum displacement (amplitude): zero KE, maximum PE. Position as function of time: x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ), where A is amplitude, ω = 2π/T is angular frequency, φ is phase constant. Key concepts: frequency, period, amplitude, equilibrium position, restoring force, damping (amplitude decreases over time due to friction), resonance (driving frequency matches natural frequency, maximum amplitude). Vocabulary: oscillation, simple harmonic motion, amplitude, period, frequency, restoring force, equilibrium, damping, resonance, angular frequency, phase. Common misconceptions: period depends on amplitude; heavier pendulums swing faster.

Physics.teks_description: Investigate wave characteristics: velocity, frequency, amplitude, wavelength relationships
Physics.prompt: This topic covers waves and sound for Physics students (TEKS Phy.8(C)). Students should investigate wave characteristics and mathematical relationships. Wave types: transverse (displacement perpendicular to propagation, e.g., light, water surface) and longitudinal (displacement parallel to propagation, e.g., sound, compression waves). Wave equation: v = fλ, where v is velocity, f is frequency, and λ is wavelength. Key properties: amplitude (maximum displacement, relates to energy/loudness), frequency (cycles per second, Hz, relates to pitch), wavelength (distance between consecutive crests), period T = 1/f. Sound waves: longitudinal mechanical waves requiring a medium; speed depends on medium (faster in solids than gases); speed in air ≈ 343 m/s at 20°C. Sound properties: pitch (frequency), loudness (amplitude), quality/timbre (waveform). Doppler effect: perceived frequency changes when source or observer moves; approaching = higher pitch, receding = lower pitch. Vocabulary: crest, trough, compression, rarefaction, wavelength, frequency, amplitude, period, medium, Doppler effect, transverse, longitudinal. Common misconceptions: sound travels in vacuum; louder sounds travel faster; the medium moves with the wave.

Physics.teks_description: Identify electric and magnetic forces in everyday applications including generators, motors, and transformers
Physics.prompt: This topic covers electromagnetism for Physics students (TEKS Phy.6(B)). Students should understand the relationship between electricity and magnetism in everyday applications. Key concepts: electric currents produce magnetic fields (Oersted's discovery), moving magnetic fields produce electric currents (Faraday's discovery). Electromagnets: coils of wire carrying current create controllable magnetic fields; strength depends on current, number of coils, and core material. Applications: electric motors (convert electrical energy to mechanical energy using magnetic force on current-carrying conductors in a magnetic field), generators (convert mechanical energy to electrical energy via electromagnetic induction), transformers (change voltage levels using two coils with a shared magnetic core; Vs/Vp = Ns/Np). Key principles: right-hand rule for direction of magnetic field around a wire, Lenz's law (induced current opposes the change causing it). Vocabulary: electromagnet, electromagnetic induction, Faraday's law, Lenz's law, motor, generator, transformer, solenoid, magnetic flux, eddy current, alternating current, direct current. Common misconceptions: magnets and electricity are unrelated; transformers create energy; permanent magnets never lose their magnetism.

Physics.teks_description: Predict electric force magnitude between objects using Coulomb's law
Physics.prompt: This topic covers electric charge and electric fields for Physics students (TEKS Phy.6(A)). Students should understand electric forces and predict their behavior using Coulomb's law. Electric charge: two types (positive and negative), like charges repel, opposite charges attract. Charge is quantized (multiples of e = 1.6 × 10^-19 C) and conserved. Charging methods: friction (triboelectric), conduction (direct contact), induction (rearrangement without contact). Coulomb's law: F = kq1q2/r², where k = 8.99 × 10^9 N·m²/C²; force is proportional to product of charges and inversely proportional to square of distance. Electric field: E = F/q = kQ/r², region around a charge where other charges experience a force; field lines point away from positive charges and toward negative charges. Key concepts: superposition principle (net field is vector sum of individual fields), conductors vs. insulators, grounding, electrostatic shielding. Vocabulary: charge, coulomb, electric field, field lines, Coulomb's law, conductor, insulator, grounding, induction, electroscope, polarization. Common misconceptions: electrons are the only charge carriers; electric field lines can cross; neutral objects have no charges.

Physics.teks_description: Calculate current, potential difference, resistance, and power using Ohm's law
Physics.prompt: This topic covers electric potential and capacitance for Physics students (TEKS Phy.6(E)). Students should understand voltage, current, resistance, and power relationships. Electric potential (voltage): V = W/q, the electric potential energy per unit charge, measured in volts (V = J/C). Potential difference drives current flow. Current: I = q/t, the rate of charge flow, measured in amperes (A). Resistance: R = V/I (Ohm's law), opposition to current flow, measured in ohms (Ω). Factors affecting resistance: material (resistivity), length (longer = more resistance), cross-sectional area (larger = less resistance), temperature. Power: P = IV = I²R = V²/R, rate of energy conversion, measured in watts (W). Electrical energy: E = Pt, measured in joules or kilowatt-hours (kWh). Capacitance: C = q/V, ability to store charge, measured in farads (F). Energy stored in capacitor: U = ½CV². Key concepts: voltage sources, electrical safety, energy efficiency, household circuits. Vocabulary: voltage, current, resistance, Ohm's law, power, capacitance, capacitor, resistivity, conductivity, potential difference. Common misconceptions: current is used up in a circuit; voltage and current are the same thing; batteries store electrons.

Physics.teks_description: Analyze and construct series and parallel circuits using schematics
Physics.prompt: This topic covers electric circuits for Physics students (TEKS Phy.6(D)). Students should analyze and construct series and parallel circuits. Series circuits: components connected end-to-end in a single path; current is the same through all components (I_total = I1 = I2); voltages add (V_total = V1 + V2); resistances add (R_total = R1 + R2); if one component fails, circuit breaks. Parallel circuits: components connected across the same two points; voltage is the same across all branches (V_total = V1 = V2); currents add (I_total = I1 + I2); reciprocal resistances add (1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2); if one branch fails, others continue. Circuit components: battery/voltage source, resistor, switch, ammeter (series), voltmeter (parallel). Kirchhoff's laws: junction rule (currents into junction = currents out), loop rule (sum of voltage changes around closed loop = 0). Key concepts: reading circuit diagrams, using multimeters, calculating equivalent resistance for combination circuits. Vocabulary: series, parallel, equivalent resistance, junction, loop, ammeter, voltmeter, short circuit, open circuit, schematic, Kirchhoff's laws. Common misconceptions: current is used up by resistors; a short circuit has high resistance.

Physics.teks_description: Identify electric and magnetic forces and fields in everyday applications
Physics.prompt: This topic covers magnetic fields and forces for Physics students (TEKS Phy.6(B)). Students should understand magnetic fields, their sources, and their interactions with moving charges and currents. Magnetic fields: produced by moving charges, current-carrying wires, and permanent magnets. Field lines: exit north pole, enter south pole, never cross. Earth has a magnetic field (geographic north is near magnetic south). Force on moving charge: F = qvB sin(θ), force is perpendicular to both velocity and field (use right-hand rule for positive charges). Force on current-carrying wire: F = BIL sin(θ), where B is field strength, I is current, L is wire length. Magnetic field from straight wire: B = μ0I/(2πr). Solenoid: coil of wire creates uniform interior field, B = μ0nI. Key concepts: right-hand rules (for force direction and field direction), magnetic domains in permanent magnets, ferromagnetic materials (iron, nickel, cobalt), diamagnetic and paramagnetic materials. Vocabulary: magnetic field, magnetic force, tesla, right-hand rule, solenoid, magnetic domain, ferromagnetic, north pole, south pole, magnetic flux. Common misconceptions: magnetic poles can be isolated (no magnetic monopoles); all metals are magnetic.

Physics.teks_description: Identify electromagnetic induction applications including generators, motors, and transformers
Physics.prompt: This topic covers electromagnetic induction for Physics students (TEKS Phy.6(B)). Students should understand how changing magnetic fields produce electric currents and their applications. Faraday's law: a changing magnetic flux through a loop induces an electromotive force (emf); emf = -NΔΦ/Δt, where N is number of loops and Φ = BA cos(θ) is magnetic flux. Lenz's law: the induced current flows in a direction that opposes the change in flux that caused it (conservation of energy). Methods to change flux: change B (field strength), change A (area of loop), change θ (angle between field and loop normal), move loop relative to field. Applications: generators (rotate coil in magnetic field to produce AC), transformers (change voltage using two coils; Vs/Vp = Ns/Np; step-up increases voltage, step-down decreases it), eddy currents (induced currents in bulk conductors, used in braking systems and metal detectors). Key concepts: AC vs. DC, mutual inductance, self-inductance, back-emf in motors. Vocabulary: electromagnetic induction, magnetic flux, Faraday's law, Lenz's law, emf, generator, transformer, eddy current, inductance, alternating current. Common misconceptions: a stationary magnet near a coil produces current; transformers work with DC.

Physics.teks_description: Describe image formation through plane mirror reflection and convex lens refraction
Physics.prompt: This topic covers light and optics for Physics students (TEKS Phy.8(G)). Students should understand image formation through reflection and refraction. Light: electromagnetic wave, travels at c = 3 × 10^8 m/s in vacuum, exhibits both wave and particle properties. Reflection: law of reflection (angle of incidence = angle of reflection). Plane mirrors: produce virtual, upright, same-size images. Curved mirrors: concave (converging) and convex (diverging); mirror equation 1/f = 1/do + 1/di; magnification M = -di/do. Refraction: bending of light when passing between media with different optical densities; Snell's law: n1 sin(θ1) = n2 sin(θ2); index of refraction n = c/v. Total internal reflection occurs when light travels from denser to less dense medium at angles greater than the critical angle. Lenses: convex (converging) and concave (diverging); thin lens equation 1/f = 1/do + 1/di. Key concepts: real vs. virtual images, focal point, ray diagrams for mirrors and lenses. Vocabulary: reflection, refraction, index of refraction, focal length, virtual image, real image, magnification, critical angle, total internal reflection, dispersion. Common misconceptions: mirrors reverse left and right; light always travels in straight lines.

Physics.teks_description: Investigate wave behaviors: reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference, Doppler effect, polarization
Physics.prompt: This topic covers reflection and refraction wave behaviors for Physics students (TEKS Phy.8(D)). Students should investigate multiple wave behaviors including reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference, the Doppler effect, and polarization. Reflection: waves bounce off boundaries; angle of incidence equals angle of reflection; fixed-end reflection inverts the wave, free-end reflection does not. Refraction: waves change direction when entering a new medium due to speed change. Diffraction: waves bend around obstacles or spread through openings; more pronounced when obstacle/opening size is comparable to wavelength. Interference: superposition of waves; constructive (waves in phase, amplitudes add) and destructive (waves out of phase, amplitudes cancel). Standing waves: result of interference between incident and reflected waves, creating nodes (no displacement) and antinodes (maximum displacement). Doppler effect: frequency shift due to relative motion between source and observer. Polarization: restricting wave vibration to one plane (only transverse waves can be polarized). Vocabulary: reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference, constructive, destructive, standing wave, node, antinode, Doppler effect, polarization, superposition. Common misconceptions: diffraction only occurs with light; destructive interference destroys energy.

Physics.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy using work-energy theorem and energy transformations
Physics.prompt: This topic covers thermodynamics for Physics students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should apply conservation of energy principles including thermal energy transformations. Laws of thermodynamics: Zeroth law (thermal equilibrium is transitive), First law (ΔU = Q - W, conservation of energy; internal energy change equals heat added minus work done), Second law (entropy of an isolated system never decreases; heat flows from hot to cold spontaneously), Third law (absolute zero cannot be reached). Key concepts: internal energy (sum of kinetic and potential energies of all molecules), temperature (measure of average kinetic energy), heat (energy transfer due to temperature difference). Work done by gas: W = PΔV (at constant pressure). Thermodynamic processes: isothermal (constant T), isobaric (constant P), isochoric/isometric (constant V), adiabatic (no heat transfer). Heat engines: convert thermal energy to work; efficiency = W/Qh = 1 - Qc/Qh; Carnot efficiency = 1 - Tc/Th. Vocabulary: internal energy, entropy, enthalpy, heat engine, refrigerator, isothermal, adiabatic, isobaric, isochoric, Carnot cycle, thermal equilibrium. Common misconceptions: cold is transferred to objects; heat and temperature are identical.

Physics.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy including thermal energy transformations and transfer
Physics.prompt: This topic covers heat transfer for Physics students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should understand how thermal energy moves through systems. Three modes of heat transfer: conduction (direct contact, molecular collisions, dominant in solids; rate depends on conductivity, area, temperature difference, and thickness: Q/t = kAΔT/L), convection (bulk fluid motion, natural convection driven by density differences from temperature, forced convection by fans/pumps; occurs in fluids), radiation (electromagnetic waves, no medium required, all objects emit radiation; Stefan-Boltzmann law: P = εσAT⁴). Specific heat: Q = mcΔT, where c is specific heat capacity; water has high specific heat (4,184 J/kg·°C). Latent heat: Q = mL, energy for phase changes without temperature change; heat of fusion (Lf) for melting/freezing, heat of vaporization (Lv) for boiling/condensing. Thermal conductors (metals, high k values) vs. insulators (wood, plastic, air, low k values). Key concepts: thermal equilibrium, calorimetry, R-value for insulation, greenhouse effect. Vocabulary: conduction, convection, radiation, specific heat, latent heat, thermal conductivity, insulator, conductor, calorimetry, thermal equilibrium, emissivity. Common misconceptions: metals are cold (they conduct heat away from hands); radiation requires a medium.

Physics.teks_description: Describe photoelectric effect and emission spectra explained by photon model
Physics.prompt: This topic covers quantum physics basics for Physics students (TEKS Phy.9(A)). Students should understand the photoelectric effect and emission spectra as evidence for the photon model. Photoelectric effect: light shining on metal surface ejects electrons; key observations that classical wave theory could not explain: (1) electrons ejected only above threshold frequency regardless of intensity, (2) kinetic energy of ejected electrons depends on frequency not intensity, (3) emission is instantaneous. Einstein's explanation: light consists of photons with energy E = hf (h = 6.626 × 10^-34 J·s is Planck's constant, f is frequency); work function φ is minimum energy to eject electron; KE_max = hf - φ. Emission spectra: atoms emit light at specific discrete frequencies when excited electrons transition to lower energy levels; each element has unique spectrum (atomic fingerprint). Bohr model: electrons occupy discrete energy levels; energy is emitted or absorbed when electrons transition between levels, E = hf. Key concepts: wave-particle duality, quantization of energy, de Broglie wavelength λ = h/mv. Vocabulary: photon, photoelectric effect, work function, threshold frequency, emission spectrum, absorption spectrum, Planck's constant, quantized, wave-particle duality. Common misconceptions: light is purely a wave; brighter light always ejects electrons.

Physics.teks_description: Provide quantum phenomena examples including Heisenberg uncertainty principle and quantum computing
Physics.prompt: This topic covers nuclear physics basics for Physics students (TEKS Phy.9(D)). Students should understand quantum phenomena and nuclear applications. Quantum principles: Heisenberg uncertainty principle (cannot simultaneously know exact position and momentum: ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2), wave-particle duality (all matter exhibits wave properties, significant only at atomic scale), quantum tunneling (particles can pass through energy barriers they classically could not overcome). Nuclear structure: nucleus contains protons and neutrons (nucleons), held together by strong nuclear force that overcomes electromagnetic repulsion at short ranges. Nuclear reactions: fission (heavy nuclei split, used in nuclear power and weapons), fusion (light nuclei combine, powers stars, potential future energy source). Mass-energy equivalence: E = mc², small mass defect in nuclei corresponds to large binding energy. Radioactivity: alpha, beta, gamma decay; half-life describes decay rate. Applications: nuclear power, medical imaging (PET scans), radiation therapy, carbon dating, quantum computing. Vocabulary: uncertainty principle, wave-particle duality, quantum tunneling, strong nuclear force, binding energy, mass defect, fission, fusion, half-life, quantum computing. Common misconceptions: quantum effects are visible at macroscopic scales; nuclear power always produces nuclear bombs.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: Investigate ecological relationships including predation, parasitism, commensalism, mutualism, competition
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers ecology and ecosystems for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS B.13(A)).
