Students should understand ecological relationships at multiple levels of organization. Key concepts: energy flow through trophic levels (producers, primary/secondary/tertiary consumers, decomposers), the 10% rule of energy transfer, food chains vs. food webs, biogeochemical cycles (carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, water, sulfur). Ecological relationships: predation (population regulation), parasitism (one benefits at other's expense), commensalism (one benefits, other unaffected), mutualism (both benefit), competition (intraspecific and interspecific affecting resource distribution). Ecosystem services: provisioning (food, water), regulating (climate, water purification), supporting (nutrient cycling, soil formation), cultural (recreation, aesthetics). Primary productivity: gross primary productivity (GPP) vs. net primary productivity (NPP = GPP - respiration); varies by biome. Keystone species and trophic cascades demonstrate how single species can regulate entire ecosystems. Vocabulary: trophic level, biomass, primary productivity, keystone species, indicator species, pioneer species, ecological succession, climax community, resilience, ecosystem services. Common misconceptions: ecosystems are static; top predators are most important.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: Explain how environmental changes affect biodiversity and ecosystem stability
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers biodiversity for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS B.13(D)). Students should understand biodiversity levels, threats, and conservation strategies. Three levels of biodiversity: genetic diversity (variation within species), species diversity (number and relative abundance of species), ecosystem diversity (variety of habitats and ecological processes). Biodiversity hotspots: regions with exceptionally high species richness and endemism facing significant habitat loss. Threats to biodiversity (HIPPCO): Habitat loss (greatest threat), Invasive species, Population growth, Pollution, Climate change, Overexploitation. Island biogeography theory: larger islands closer to mainland support more species; applies to habitat fragments. Species-area relationship: S = cA^z. Key concepts: extinction rates (background vs. mass extinction), endangered vs. threatened species, endemic species, ecological redundancy, edge effects in habitat fragmentation. Conservation strategies: protected areas, habitat corridors, captive breeding, seed banks, legislation (Endangered Species Act, CITES). Vocabulary: biodiversity, endemic, invasive species, habitat fragmentation, edge effect, extinction, speciation, HIPPCO, conservation biology, flagship species, umbrella species. Common misconceptions: biodiversity only matters in rainforests; extinction is always caused by humans.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: Analyze disruptions to matter cycling and energy flow through trophic levels
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers population ecology for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS B.13(B)). Students should analyze population dynamics and energy flow through ecological systems. Population growth models: exponential (J-curve, dN/dt = rN, unlimited resources) vs. logistic (S-curve, dN/dt = rN(K-N)/K, resources limit growth). Carrying capacity (K): maximum population size an environment can sustain. r-strategists vs. K-strategists: r-selected species (many offspring, little parental care, short lifespan) vs. K-selected species (few offspring, extensive parental care, long lifespan). Survivorship curves: Type I (low early mortality, high late), Type II (constant mortality), Type III (high early mortality). Density-dependent factors: competition, predation, disease, parasitism. Density-independent factors: natural disasters, climate events. Age structure diagrams: predict future growth (expanding, stable, declining populations). Demographic transition model: pre-industrial to post-industrial population patterns. Trophic levels: energy decreases at each level (ecological pyramids of energy, biomass, numbers). Vocabulary: birth rate, death rate, immigration, emigration, carrying capacity, r-strategist, K-strategist, age structure, demographic transition, total fertility rate, doubling time. Common misconceptions: human population can grow indefinitely; K is fixed.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Evaluate the role of water resources and impacts of pollution on aquatic systems
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers water resources and pollution for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.10(A)). Students should evaluate water resource management and pollution impacts on aquatic systems. Water distribution: 97.5% saltwater, 2.5% freshwater (most in ice caps and glaciers); only ~1% accessible for human use. Key concepts: groundwater (aquifers, water table, recharge zones), surface water (lakes, rivers, wetlands), watershed management. Water pollution types: point source (identifiable origin, e.g., factory discharge) vs. nonpoint source (diffuse, e.g., agricultural runoff). Major pollutants: nutrients (nitrogen, phosphorus causing eutrophication and algal blooms), pathogens, heavy metals (mercury biomagnification), thermal pollution, sediment, synthetic chemicals (pesticides, pharmaceuticals), plastics. Eutrophication process: excess nutrients → algal bloom → decomposition → oxygen depletion → dead zone. Water treatment: primary (physical), secondary (biological), tertiary (chemical/advanced). Key issues: groundwater depletion (Ogallala Aquifer), ocean acidification, wetland destruction, dam impacts, water scarcity and conflict. Vocabulary: aquifer, watershed, eutrophication, dead zone, hypoxia, point source, nonpoint source, BOD, dissolved oxygen, desalination, water table, potable. Common misconceptions: water is a limitless resource; groundwater flows like underground rivers.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Analyze sources, effects, and mitigation of air pollution
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers air pollution for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.10(B)). Students should analyze sources, effects, and mitigation strategies for air pollutants. Primary pollutants: emitted directly from sources (CO, SO2, NOx, particulate matter PM, VOCs, lead). Secondary pollutants: formed by chemical reactions in atmosphere (ozone O3, sulfuric acid, nitric acid, PAN, photochemical smog). Major issues: smog (industrial/London smog from SO2 and particulates; photochemical/LA smog from NOx and VOCs reacting in sunlight), acid deposition (SO2 and NOx combine with water to form sulfuric and nitric acid; pH < 5.6; damages aquatic ecosystems, forests, buildings), indoor air pollution (radon, CO, formaldehyde, asbestos, mold, tobacco smoke; often worse than outdoor in developing nations from cooking fires). Clean Air Act: regulates criteria pollutants (CO, SO2, NO2, O3, PM, Pb), established NAAQS. Mitigation strategies: catalytic converters, scrubbers, electrostatic precipitators, cap-and-trade programs, alternative energy, emission standards. Air Quality Index (AQI) measures pollution levels. Vocabulary: criteria pollutant, primary pollutant, secondary pollutant, smog, acid deposition, temperature inversion, VOC, particulate matter, catalytic converter, scrubber. Common misconceptions: all ozone is bad (stratospheric ozone is protective); clean-looking air is pollution-free.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Evaluate evidence and models related to global climate change
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers climate change for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.10(C)). Students should evaluate evidence and models related to global climate change. Greenhouse effect: natural process where atmospheric gases (CO2, CH4, N2O, H2O vapor, CFCs) trap infrared radiation, warming Earth's surface. Enhanced greenhouse effect: human activities increase greenhouse gas concentrations, amplifying warming. Evidence for climate change: rising global temperatures, melting ice sheets and glaciers, sea level rise, ocean acidification, shifting species ranges, increased extreme weather events, ice core data (CO2 and temperature correlation over 800,000 years). Carbon cycle disruption: burning fossil fuels, deforestation, cement production increase atmospheric CO2 from ~280 ppm (pre-industrial) to over 420 ppm. Climate models: computer simulations predicting future scenarios based on emission pathways (IPCC scenarios). Impacts: sea level rise threatening coastal areas, coral bleaching, changes in precipitation patterns, agricultural disruption, biodiversity loss, climate refugees. Mitigation: reduce emissions (renewable energy, efficiency, carbon tax), carbon capture and storage. Adaptation: sea walls, drought-resistant crops, infrastructure planning. Vocabulary: greenhouse gas, radiative forcing, albedo, carbon sink, feedback loop, IPCC, mitigation, adaptation, carbon footprint, ocean acidification. Common misconceptions: weather disproves climate change; the greenhouse effect is entirely human-caused.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Analyze fossil fuel formation, extraction, and environmental impacts
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers fossil fuels and energy production for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.9(A)). Students should analyze fossil fuel formation, extraction, and environmental impacts. Fossil fuels: coal (formed from ancient plant material in swamps, most polluting), petroleum/oil (formed from marine organisms, used for transportation), natural gas (methane CH4, cleanest fossil fuel, often found with oil). Formation: millions of years of heat and pressure on organic matter. Extraction methods: surface mining (strip mining, mountaintop removal), subsurface mining, conventional drilling, hydraulic fracturing (fracking), offshore drilling, tar sands extraction. Environmental impacts: air pollution (CO2, SO2, NOx, particulates), water pollution (acid mine drainage, oil spills, fracking chemicals), habitat destruction, land subsidence, greenhouse gas emissions driving climate change. Energy concepts: net energy (EROI - energy returned on energy invested), energy efficiency, capacity factor. Coal-fired power plants: combustion heats water → steam → turbine → generator. Nuclear energy: fission of uranium-235, high energy density, radioactive waste concerns, meltdown risk, no direct CO2 emissions. Vocabulary: fossil fuel, nonrenewable, EROI, fracking, acid mine drainage, mountaintop removal, tar sands, carbon capture, energy density, capacity factor. Common misconceptions: fossil fuels will last forever; natural gas is completely clean.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Compare renewable and nonrenewable energy sources
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers renewable energy sources for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.9(B)). Students should compare renewable and nonrenewable energy sources, analyzing benefits and limitations. Solar energy: photovoltaic cells convert sunlight to electricity; passive solar design; concentrated solar power; intermittent, requires storage, declining costs. Wind energy: turbines convert kinetic energy; onshore and offshore; bird/bat mortality, visual impact, intermittent. Hydroelectric: dams convert flowing water's potential energy; reliable, but habitat disruption, sediment trapping, methane from reservoirs. Biomass/biofuels: ethanol from corn/sugarcane, biodiesel, wood; carbon neutral in theory but land use concerns. Geothermal: heat from Earth's interior; limited to tectonically active areas, reliable baseload power. Hydrogen fuel cells: H2 + O2 → H2O + electricity; clean but production often uses fossil fuels. Tidal and wave energy: emerging technologies. Key concepts: energy efficiency, grid integration, energy storage (batteries, pumped hydro), life-cycle analysis, distributed generation, net metering, feed-in tariffs, carbon neutrality, LCOE (levelized cost of energy). Vocabulary: renewable, photovoltaic, turbine, hydroelectric, biomass, geothermal, hydrogen fuel cell, intermittent, baseload, capacity factor, LCOE, net metering. Common misconceptions: renewable means zero environmental impact; solar panels don't work on cloudy days.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Evaluate waste management strategies including recycling and reduction
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers waste management for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.10(D)). Students should evaluate waste management strategies and their environmental implications. Waste hierarchy (most to least preferred): reduce, reuse, recycle, recover (energy), dispose. Municipal solid waste (MSW): household and commercial waste; composition varies by country (paper, food, plastics, metals, glass). Disposal methods: sanitary landfills (lined, leachate collection, methane capture; issues include groundwater contamination, space limitations), incineration (reduces volume by 90%, energy recovery possible; produces ash, air pollutants, requires scrubbers), open dumps (unregulated, common in developing nations). Recycling: closed-loop vs. open-loop, contamination issues, markets for recyclables, e-waste recycling challenges. Hazardous waste: toxic, flammable, corrosive, or reactive; RCRA regulates cradle-to-grave management; Superfund (CERCLA) cleans contaminated sites. Composting: aerobic decomposition of organic waste into soil amendment. Industrial ecology: waste from one process becomes input for another (circular economy). Vocabulary: MSW, landfill, leachate, incineration, recycling, composting, hazardous waste, e-waste, RCRA, Superfund, circular economy, life cycle analysis, source reduction. Common misconceptions: recycling solves the waste problem; biodegradable items decompose quickly in landfills.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Analyze soil composition and agricultural practices
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers soil and agriculture for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.8(A)). Students should analyze soil composition and agricultural practices. Soil composition: minerals (45%), water (25%), air (25%), organic matter (5%); formed from weathering of parent rock and decomposition of organic material. Soil horizons: O (organic), A (topsoil, most fertile), B (subsoil, mineral accumulation), C (weathered parent material), R (bedrock). Soil properties: texture (sand, silt, clay proportions), structure, porosity, permeability, pH, cation exchange capacity. Agricultural practices: conventional (monoculture, synthetic fertilizers, pesticides, irrigation, mechanization) vs. sustainable (crop rotation, cover crops, integrated pest management, organic farming, no-till agriculture, agroforestry). Soil degradation: erosion (wind and water), compaction, salinization (from irrigation), desertification, loss of fertility, contamination. Green revolution: high-yield varieties, irrigation, fertilizers, pesticides; increased food production but environmental costs. Food security: population growth vs. food production, food distribution inequality. Vocabulary: humus, loam, topsoil, erosion, desertification, salinization, monoculture, polyculture, IPM, organic farming, GMO, green revolution, food security, agroforestry. Common misconceptions: soil is just dirt; organic farming cannot feed the world.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Evaluate land use practices and conservation strategies
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers land use and conservation for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.8(B)). Students should evaluate land use practices and conservation strategies. Land use categories: urban/suburban, agricultural, forestry, mining, recreation, wilderness. Urban sprawl: low-density development, car dependency, loss of farmland and habitat, increased impervious surfaces affecting water runoff. Smart growth: compact development, mixed-use zoning, public transit, green spaces. Forest management: clear-cutting (efficient but destructive), selective cutting (less impact), prescribed burns (reduce wildfire risk, ecological benefits), sustainable forestry certification (FSC). Public lands: national parks (preservation), national forests (multiple use), wildlife refuges, BLM lands, wilderness areas. Conservation approaches: preservation (keeping nature undisturbed) vs. conservation (sustainable use of resources). Protected areas: IUCN categories, marine protected areas, wildlife corridors connecting habitat fragments. Ecological restoration: returning degraded ecosystems to natural state; wetland restoration, stream restoration, prairie restoration. Land use planning tools: zoning, environmental impact assessments, conservation easements. Vocabulary: urban sprawl, smart growth, clear-cutting, selective cutting, multiple use, preservation, conservation, restoration, easement, impervious surface, greenway, buffer zone. Common misconceptions: national parks and national forests have the same purpose; development always destroys ecosystems.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Analyze environmental legislation and regulatory frameworks
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers environmental laws and regulations for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.11(A)). Students should analyze major environmental legislation and regulatory frameworks. Key U.S. environmental laws: NEPA (1970, requires Environmental Impact Statements for federal projects), Clean Air Act (1970, regulates air emissions, criteria pollutants, NAAQS), Clean Water Act (1972, regulates point source discharges, NPDES permits, wetland protection), Endangered Species Act (1973, protects threatened and endangered species and critical habitats), RCRA (1976, regulates hazardous waste cradle-to-grave), CERCLA/Superfund (1980, cleans up contaminated sites), TSCA (regulates chemical substances), Safe Drinking Water Act (sets maximum contaminant levels). Regulatory agencies: EPA (federal enforcement), state environmental agencies. International agreements: Montreal Protocol (successfully phased out CFCs), Kyoto Protocol (greenhouse gas reduction targets), Paris Agreement (limit warming to 1.5-2°C). Key concepts: command-and-control regulation vs. market-based approaches (cap-and-trade, carbon tax, green subsidies), cost-benefit analysis, precautionary principle, environmental justice. Vocabulary: NEPA, EIS, NAAQS, NPDES, EPA, cap-and-trade, precautionary principle, environmental justice, regulation, compliance, enforcement, mitigation. Common misconceptions: environmental laws prevent all pollution; international agreements are legally binding and enforceable.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Evaluate the effects of toxins and pollutants on ecosystems and human health
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers environmental toxicology for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.10(E)). Students should evaluate the effects of toxins and pollutants on ecosystems and human health. Key concepts: dose-response relationship (the dose makes the poison), LD50 (lethal dose for 50% of test population), threshold dose, acute vs. chronic exposure, synergistic effects (combined effect greater than sum of individual effects). Bioaccumulation: buildup of toxins in an individual organism over time. Biomagnification: increasing concentration of toxins through trophic levels (e.g., DDT in birds of prey, mercury in fish). Persistent organic pollutants (POPs): resist degradation, bioaccumulate (DDT, PCBs, dioxins). Endocrine disruptors: mimic or block hormones (BPA, phthalates, atrazine); affect reproduction and development at very low doses. Heavy metals: lead (neurological damage), mercury (Minamata disease), arsenic, cadmium. Risk assessment: hazard identification, dose-response assessment, exposure assessment, risk characterization. Routes of exposure: ingestion, inhalation, dermal contact. Vulnerable populations: children, elderly, pregnant women, low-income communities. Vocabulary: toxin, toxicant, dose-response, LD50, bioaccumulation, biomagnification, persistent organic pollutant, endocrine disruptor, carcinogen, mutagen, teratogen, risk assessment. Common misconceptions: natural substances are safe; any amount of a chemical is dangerous.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Analyze human population dynamics and demographic trends
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers human population growth for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.7(A)). Students should analyze human population dynamics, demographic trends, and their environmental implications. Current global population: approximately 8 billion; growth rate declining but population still increasing. Demographic transition model: Stage 1 (high birth and death rates, stable population), Stage 2 (death rate falls, rapid growth), Stage 3 (birth rate falls, growth slows), Stage 4 (low birth and death rates, stable), Stage 5 (birth rate below death rate, declining). Key indicators: crude birth rate (CBR), crude death rate (CDR), total fertility rate (TFR, replacement level ≈ 2.1), infant mortality rate, life expectancy, doubling time (Rule of 70: doubling time = 70/growth rate%). Age structure diagrams: expanding (triangular, growing populations in developing nations), stable (columnar), contracting (inverted, aging populations in developed nations). Factors affecting growth: access to education (especially women's education), contraception availability, economic development, cultural and religious values, government policies (China's one-child policy, pronatalist policies). Environmental impacts: resource consumption, ecological footprint, IPAT model (Impact = Population × Affluence × Technology). Vocabulary: demographic transition, TFR, CBR, CDR, infant mortality, doubling time, age structure, ecological footprint, IPAT, urbanization, migration. Common misconceptions: overpopulation is solely a developing-world problem; simply reducing population solves environmental issues.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Evaluate sustainability practices and their environmental impacts
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers sustainability for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.11(B)). Students should evaluate sustainability practices and their environmental impacts. Sustainability definition: meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet their needs (Brundtland Commission, 1987). Three pillars: environmental (ecological integrity), economic (viable economy), social (social equity). Key concepts: ecological footprint (amount of land/water needed to support a person's lifestyle), carrying capacity, renewable vs. nonrenewable resources, natural capital (ecosystem services, biodiversity, resources). Sustainable practices: sustainable agriculture (organic farming, IPM, crop rotation, permaculture), sustainable energy (renewables, energy efficiency), sustainable forestry (FSC certification), green building (LEED certification, energy-efficient design), sustainable transportation (public transit, EV, bike infrastructure). Circular economy: design out waste, keep products and materials in use, regenerate natural systems; contrast with linear economy (take-make-dispose). Indicators: UN Sustainable Development Goals (17 SDGs), Human Development Index, Genuine Progress Indicator vs. GDP. Vocabulary: sustainability, ecological footprint, natural capital, circular economy, LEED, cradle-to-cradle, life cycle assessment, triple bottom line, SDGs, carrying capacity, renewable, nonrenewable. Common misconceptions: sustainability means going without; economic growth and sustainability are incompatible.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Evaluate evidence related to global warming and greenhouse effect
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers global warming and the greenhouse effect for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.10(C)). Students should evaluate evidence related to global warming mechanisms and impacts. Greenhouse effect mechanism: solar radiation passes through atmosphere → Earth's surface absorbs and re-emits as infrared radiation → greenhouse gases absorb and re-emit IR radiation → warming of lower atmosphere and surface. Major greenhouse gases ranked by contribution: water vapor (most abundant, natural feedback), CO2 (fossil fuels, deforestation; ~420 ppm and rising), methane CH4 (25x more potent than CO2 per molecule; sources: livestock, rice paddies, landfills, natural gas), nitrous oxide N2O (agriculture, fossil fuels), CFCs (synthetic, also destroy ozone). Global warming potential (GWP): compares warming effect of different gases relative to CO2 over 100 years. Evidence: instrumental temperature records show ~1.1°C warming since pre-industrial times, ice core records show CO2-temperature correlation, satellite data, ocean heat content, retreating glaciers. Positive feedback loops: ice-albedo feedback (melting ice reduces reflectivity → more warming), permafrost thaw (releases CH4), water vapor feedback. Negative feedbacks: increased plant growth (absorbs CO2), cloud formation. Vocabulary: greenhouse effect, greenhouse gas, GWP, radiative forcing, albedo, feedback loop, ice core, carbon dioxide equivalent, forcing agent, climate sensitivity. Common misconceptions: the greenhouse effect is unnatural; CO2 is the only greenhouse gas.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Analyze atmospheric changes including ozone depletion
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers ozone depletion for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.10(B)). Students should understand stratospheric ozone chemistry and distinguish ozone depletion from global warming. Ozone layer: concentrated in stratosphere (15-35 km altitude); absorbs harmful UV-B and UV-C radiation, protecting life on Earth. Ozone formation: O2 + UV → 2O, then O + O2 → O3 (Chapman cycle). Ozone destruction by CFCs: UV breaks C-Cl bond in CFCs releasing chlorine radical; Cl + O3 → ClO + O2, then ClO + O → Cl + O2; one chlorine atom can destroy ~100,000 ozone molecules (catalytic cycle). Ozone hole: severe thinning over Antarctica during spring (September-November) due to polar stratospheric clouds facilitating CFC-ozone reactions. Effects of ozone depletion: increased UV-B reaching surface causing skin cancer, cataracts, immune suppression, damage to phytoplankton, crop damage. Montreal Protocol (1987): international agreement phasing out CFCs and other ozone-depleting substances; considered most successful environmental treaty; ozone layer recovering slowly. Alternatives: HCFCs (transitional, less destructive), HFCs (no ozone impact but potent greenhouse gases). Vocabulary: stratosphere, ozone layer, CFC, UV radiation, catalytic cycle, ozone hole, Montreal Protocol, polar stratospheric cloud, chlorine radical, HCFC, HFC. Common misconceptions: the ozone hole causes global warming; the ozone hole is a permanent gap; spray cans still contain CFCs.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Analyze impacts of deforestation on ecosystems and climate
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers deforestation for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.8(C)). Students should analyze the impacts of deforestation on ecosystems, climate, and biodiversity. Causes of deforestation: agricultural expansion (cattle ranching, soy and palm oil plantations, slash-and-burn farming), logging (legal and illegal), mining, urbanization, infrastructure development, fuelwood collection. Scale: approximately 10 million hectares lost annually, primarily in tropical regions (Amazon, Congo Basin, Southeast Asia). Environmental impacts: biodiversity loss (tropical forests contain over 50% of terrestrial species), climate change (forests store ~25% of global carbon; deforestation releases CO2 and reduces carbon sequestration capacity), disruption of water cycle (reduced transpiration leads to decreased rainfall), soil erosion and degradation (tree roots stabilize soil; removal leads to erosion, landslides, sedimentation), flooding (reduced water absorption), habitat fragmentation. Social impacts: displacement of indigenous communities, loss of traditional knowledge, short-term economic gains vs. long-term ecological costs. Solutions: reforestation and afforestation, sustainable forestry practices (selective logging, certification), agroforestry, protected areas, REDD+ (Reducing Emissions from Deforestation and forest Degradation), consumer awareness (sustainable palm oil, FSC-certified wood), payments for ecosystem services. Vocabulary: deforestation, reforestation, afforestation, carbon sequestration, slash-and-burn, selective logging, REDD+, agroforestry, habitat fragmentation, old-growth forest. Common misconceptions: planting trees fully offsets deforestation; all logging is bad.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Evaluate environmental impacts of urban development
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers urbanization for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.8(D)). Students should evaluate the environmental impacts of urban development and strategies for sustainable cities. Urbanization trends: over 55% of world population lives in cities (projected 68% by 2050); fastest growth in developing nations; megacities (10+ million people). Environmental impacts: heat island effect (cities 1-3°C warmer due to concrete, asphalt, reduced vegetation, waste heat), increased impervious surfaces (increased stormwater runoff, flooding, water pollution), air pollution (vehicle emissions, industrial sources, smog), water pollution (runoff carrying oil, heavy metals, nutrients), habitat destruction and fragmentation, increased resource consumption and waste generation, noise and light pollution. Urban sprawl: low-density, automobile-dependent development consuming farmland and natural habitats; increases driving, energy use, and infrastructure costs. Smart growth strategies: mixed-use development, compact building design, walkable neighborhoods, public transit investment, urban growth boundaries, green spaces and parks, infill development, transit-oriented development. Green infrastructure: green roofs, rain gardens, permeable pavement, urban forests, bioswales reduce runoff and cool cities. Vocabulary: urbanization, urban sprawl, smart growth, heat island, impervious surface, infill, green infrastructure, transit-oriented development, megacity, brownfield, greenfield. Common misconceptions: cities are always worse for the environment than suburbs; urbanization cannot be sustainable.

AP®︎/College Environmental Science.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Analyze ethical perspectives on environmental issues
AP®︎/College Environmental Science.prompt: This topic covers environmental ethics for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.11(C)). Students should analyze ethical perspectives on environmental issues and their influence on policy. Major ethical frameworks: anthropocentrism (human-centered, nature valued for human benefit; supports resource management for sustained yield), biocentrism (all living things have inherent value; opposes actions harming individual organisms), ecocentrism (ecosystems and ecological processes have intrinsic value; Aldo Leopold's land ethic: 'A thing is right when it tends to preserve the integrity, stability, and beauty of the biotic community'). Key environmental philosophies: preservation ethic (keep nature undisturbed, John Muir), conservation ethic (wise use of resources, Gifford Pinchot), deep ecology (radical restructuring of human-nature relationship), ecofeminism (links environmental degradation to patriarchal systems), environmental justice (fair treatment regardless of race, income, or nationality). Tragedy of the commons (Garrett Hardin): shared resources are overexploited when individuals act in self-interest; solutions include regulation, privatization, and community management. Intergenerational equity: responsibility to future generations. Key debates: economic development vs. environmental protection, rights of nature, global vs. local responsibility. Vocabulary: anthropocentrism, biocentrism, ecocentrism, land ethic, preservation, conservation, environmental justice, tragedy of the commons, intrinsic value, instrumental value, stewardship, precautionary principle. Common misconceptions: environmentalism is anti-human; ethics and science are separate.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Describe one-dimensional motion using distance, displacement, speed, velocity, and acceleration
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers kinematics for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.5(C)). Students should describe and analyze motion quantitatively using kinematic equations. Kinematic equations (constant acceleration): v = v0 + at, x = x0 + v0t + ½at², v² = v0² + 2a(x - x0), x = ½(v + v0)t. Key distinctions: distance (scalar, total path length) vs. displacement (vector, change in position); speed (scalar) vs. velocity (vector); average vs. instantaneous quantities. Motion graphs: position-time (slope = velocity, curvature indicates acceleration), velocity-time (slope = acceleration, area under curve = displacement), acceleration-time (area = change in velocity). Multi-dimensional motion: resolve vectors into components, treat each dimension independently. Free fall: a = g = 9.8 m/s² downward. Problem-solving strategy: identify known/unknown quantities, select appropriate kinematic equation, solve algebraically before substituting numbers, check units and reasonableness. Vocabulary: kinematics, displacement, velocity, acceleration, scalar, vector, component, projectile, free fall, reference frame, uniform motion. AP exam focus: qualitative reasoning about motion graphs, ranking tasks, multi-step problems connecting kinematics to dynamics. Common misconceptions: acceleration and velocity always point in the same direction; zero velocity means zero acceleration.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Calculate force effects using free body diagrams and Newton's second law
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers dynamics (forces and Newton's laws) for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.5(F)). Students should analyze forces quantitatively using free body diagrams and Newton's laws. Newton's First Law: objects maintain their state of motion unless a net external force acts (inertia). Newton's Second Law: ΣF = ma (vector equation); net force equals mass times acceleration. Newton's Third Law: forces come in action-reaction pairs (equal magnitude, opposite direction, on different objects). Free body diagram procedure: identify the system, draw all external forces acting ON the object (gravity, normal, friction, tension, applied, spring). Common force types: gravitational (Fg = mg), normal (perpendicular to surface), kinetic friction (fk = μkN), static friction (fs ≤ μsN), tension, spring (F = -kx). Problem-solving: draw FBD, choose coordinate system (often align with acceleration), write ΣFx = max and ΣFy = may, solve system of equations. Inclined planes: decompose gravity into components parallel and perpendicular to surface. Atwood machines and pulley systems: apply Newton's second law to each mass separately. Vocabulary: force, net force, inertia, mass, weight, normal force, friction, tension, equilibrium, free body diagram, coefficient of friction. Common misconceptions: force is needed to maintain motion; Newton's third law pairs cancel out.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Describe and analyze uniform circular and projectile motion; Phy.5(H) gravitational force
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers circular motion and gravitation for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.5(D)). Students should analyze objects moving in circular paths and gravitational interactions. Uniform circular motion: constant speed, changing velocity direction, centripetal acceleration ac = v²/r directed toward center. Centripetal force: Fc = mv²/r is the net force toward the center (provided by tension, gravity, friction, normal force, or a combination). Period and frequency: T = 2πr/v, f = 1/T, v = 2πr/T. Newton's law of universal gravitation: Fg = Gm1m2/r², where G = 6.674 × 10^-11 N·m²/kg². Gravitational field: g = GM/r². Orbital motion: gravitational force provides centripetal force (GMm/r² = mv²/r), leading to v = √(GM/r). Kepler's third law: T² ∝ r³. Key concepts: apparent weightlessness in orbit (free fall), geosynchronous orbits, escape speed, gravitational potential energy U = -GMm/r. Banked curves: component of normal force provides centripetal force. Conical pendulum: tension component provides centripetal force. Vocabulary: centripetal, centrifugal, period, frequency, orbit, satellite, gravitational field, geosynchronous, escape speed, banked curve. Common misconceptions: there is no gravity in space; centrifugal force is real in an inertial frame; satellites are beyond Earth's gravity.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Investigate and calculate mechanical, kinetic, and potential energy
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers energy for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.7(B)). Students should investigate and calculate mechanical, kinetic, and potential energy and apply conservation principles. Forms of mechanical energy: kinetic energy KE = ½mv², gravitational potential energy PE = mgh (near surface) or U = -GMm/r (general), elastic potential energy PE = ½kx². Conservation of mechanical energy: in absence of non-conservative forces, KEi + PEi = KEf + PEf. Work-energy theorem: Wnet = ΔKE. Work: W = Fd cos(θ), area under F vs. d graph. Power: P = W/t = Fv, measured in watts. Energy bar charts: visual representation of energy transformations at different points in a process. Non-conservative forces (friction): convert mechanical energy to thermal energy; total energy is still conserved but mechanical energy decreases. Springs: Hooke's law F = -kx; elastic PE = ½kx². Key concepts: reference point for PE is arbitrary (choose convenient zero), energy is scalar (no direction), identify system boundaries for conservation analysis. Vocabulary: kinetic energy, potential energy, mechanical energy, conservation of energy, work, power, work-energy theorem, dissipation, internal energy, system, conservative force. Common misconceptions: energy is a substance; KE is always conserved in collisions; potential energy belongs to a single object rather than a system.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Calculate impulse and momentum in physical systems
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers momentum for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.7(D)). Students should calculate impulse and momentum and apply conservation of momentum. Linear momentum: p = mv, a vector quantity (has direction). Impulse: J = FΔt = Δp; area under F vs. t graph equals impulse. Impulse-momentum theorem: net impulse on an object equals its change in momentum. Conservation of momentum: in an isolated system (no net external force), total momentum is conserved: Σpi = Σpf. Collision types: elastic (both momentum and KE conserved), inelastic (momentum conserved, KE not), perfectly inelastic (objects stick together, maximum KE loss). Explosion: reverse of perfectly inelastic collision; total momentum of fragments equals initial momentum (often zero). Two-dimensional collisions: apply conservation of momentum independently in x and y directions. Center of mass: xcm = Σmixi/Σmi; moves as if all mass were concentrated at that point with Fnet = Mtotal × acm. Key concepts: momentum is conserved even when energy is not, identifying the system is crucial for applying conservation laws. Vocabulary: momentum, impulse, conservation of momentum, elastic collision, inelastic collision, perfectly inelastic, recoil, center of mass, isolated system. Common misconceptions: momentum and kinetic energy are the same; heavier objects always have more momentum.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Examine simple harmonic motion and wave energy propagation in various media
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers simple harmonic motion (SHM) for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.8(A)). Students should analyze oscillatory systems and energy in SHM. Definition: SHM occurs when restoring force is proportional to displacement (F = -kx for spring, F = -mg sin(θ) ≈ -mgθ/L for small-angle pendulum). Mass-spring system: T = 2π√(m/k); period depends on mass and spring constant, NOT amplitude. Simple pendulum: T = 2π√(L/g); period depends on length and gravitational field, NOT mass or amplitude (for small angles). Energy in SHM: total energy E = ½kA² is constant; at equilibrium, all KE (maximum velocity); at amplitude, all PE (zero velocity). Velocity at any position: v = ±√(k/m)(A² - x²). Position function: x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ) where ω = 2π/T. Connections between SHM and circular motion: SHM is the projection of uniform circular motion onto one axis. Key concepts: amplitude, period, and frequency are independent in ideal SHM; damping reduces amplitude over time; driven oscillations and resonance (maximum amplitude when driving frequency equals natural frequency). Vocabulary: oscillation, amplitude, period, frequency, restoring force, equilibrium, angular frequency, phase, damping, resonance, natural frequency. Common misconceptions: larger amplitude means longer period; heavier pendulums have different periods.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Describe and analyze rotational and circular motion in two dimensions
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers torque and rotational motion for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.5(D)). Students should analyze rotational dynamics as parallel to translational dynamics. Torque: τ = rF sin(θ) = r⊥F, the rotational equivalent of force; measured in N·m. Sign convention: counterclockwise is typically positive. Rotational kinematics: angular displacement θ, angular velocity ω, angular acceleration α; analogous equations to linear kinematics (θ = ω0t + ½αt², ω = ω0 + αt, ω² = ω0² + 2αθ). Newton's second law for rotation: Στ = Iα, where I is moment of inertia (rotational equivalent of mass). Moment of inertia: I = Σmr² (depends on mass distribution relative to axis); common shapes: point mass I = mr², disk I = ½MR², hoop I = MR², rod about center I = (1/12)ML². Rotational kinetic energy: KErot = ½Iω². Rolling without slipping: v = ωr, total KE = ½mv² + ½Iω². Angular momentum: L = Iω; conserved when no net external torque (ice skater pulling arms in). Static equilibrium: ΣF = 0 and Στ = 0. Vocabulary: torque, moment of inertia, angular velocity, angular acceleration, angular momentum, rotational inertia, lever arm, axis of rotation, rolling, precession. Common misconceptions: torque and force are the same; heavier objects always have more rotational inertia.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Compare transverse and longitudinal waves, including electromagnetic and sound waves
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers mechanical waves and sound for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.8(B)). Students should compare transverse and longitudinal waves and analyze their properties. Mechanical waves require a medium for propagation. Transverse waves: particle displacement perpendicular to wave direction (strings, water surface). Longitudinal waves: particle displacement parallel to wave direction (sound, compression springs). Wave equation: v = fλ. Wave speed depends on medium properties: string v = √(T/μ) where T is tension and μ is linear mass density; sound speed depends on medium density and elasticity (faster in solids than gases). Sound waves: longitudinal pressure waves; speed in air ≈ 343 m/s at 20°C; frequency determines pitch, amplitude determines loudness, waveform determines timbre. Intensity: I = P/A, measured in W/m²; decibel scale is logarithmic. Standing waves on strings: fixed-end boundary conditions create nodes and antinodes; harmonics fn = nv/(2L) for both ends fixed. Standing waves in pipes: open end = antinode, closed end = node. Beat frequency: fbeat = |f1 - f2|. Doppler effect: f' = f(v ± vo)/(v ∓ vs). Vocabulary: transverse, longitudinal, compression, rarefaction, amplitude, frequency, wavelength, standing wave, harmonic, node, antinode, resonance, beats, Doppler effect. Common misconceptions: sound is transverse; louder sounds are higher pitched.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Predict electric force magnitude between objects using Coulomb's law
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers electrostatics for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.6(A)). Students should analyze electric forces and fields using Coulomb's law. Electric charge: fundamental property of matter; positive (protons) and negative (electrons); charge is quantized (q = ne, where e = 1.6 × 10^-19 C) and conserved. Coulomb's law: F = kq1q2/r², where k = 8.99 × 10^9 N·m²/C²; attractive between opposite charges, repulsive between like charges; follows inverse-square law (analogous to gravity). Electric field: E = F/q = kQ/r²; vector quantity pointing in the direction a positive test charge would accelerate. Field lines: originate on positive charges, terminate on negative charges; density indicates field strength; never cross. Superposition: net force or field is the vector sum of individual contributions. Charging methods: friction, conduction, induction. Conductors vs. insulators: in conductors, charges move freely and excess charge resides on surface; in electrostatic equilibrium, E = 0 inside conductor. Key concepts: conservation of charge, Coulomb's law vs. gravitational law comparison, point charge model. Vocabulary: charge, coulomb, electric field, field lines, Coulomb's law, conductor, insulator, grounding, polarization, superposition, electrostatic equilibrium. Common misconceptions: electric field exists only between charges; neutral objects cannot be attracted to charged objects.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Analyze and construct series and parallel circuits using schematics
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers DC circuits for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.6(D)). Students should analyze series and parallel circuits quantitatively. Circuit fundamentals: current I = ΔQ/Δt (charge flow rate, amperes), voltage V (potential difference, volts), resistance R (opposition to current, ohms). Ohm's law: V = IR. Resistors in series: Req = R1 + R2 + ...; same current through all, voltages add. Resistors in parallel: 1/Req = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + ...; same voltage across all, currents add. Kirchhoff's junction rule: ΣI_in = ΣI_out (conservation of charge). Kirchhoff's loop rule: ΣΔV = 0 around any closed loop (conservation of energy). Power: P = IV = I²R = V²/R. Internal resistance: real batteries have internal resistance r; terminal voltage V = emf - Ir. Circuit analysis: draw circuit diagrams, identify series and parallel combinations, apply Kirchhoff's rules for complex circuits. Ammeter: low resistance, connected in series. Voltmeter: high resistance, connected in parallel. Key concepts: brightness of bulbs relates to power dissipated, short circuits, equivalent resistance of combination circuits. Vocabulary: current, voltage, resistance, Ohm's law, series, parallel, equivalent resistance, Kirchhoff's laws, EMF, internal resistance, ammeter, voltmeter, power. Common misconceptions: current is used up in resistors; batteries provide electrons; current flows from negative to positive.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Calculate current, potential difference, resistance, and power using Ohm's law
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers simple circuits and Ohm's law for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.6(E)). Students should calculate current, potential difference, resistance, and power using fundamental circuit relationships. Ohm's law: V = IR relates voltage (potential difference across component), current (through component), and resistance. Ohmic vs. non-ohmic materials: ohmic materials (metals at constant temperature) show linear V-I relationship; non-ohmic (diodes, filament bulbs) do not. Resistance factors: R = ρL/A, where ρ is resistivity (material property), L is length, A is cross-sectional area; resistance increases with temperature for metals. Power in circuits: P = IV = I²R = V²/R; measured in watts. Electrical energy: E = Pt; household energy measured in kilowatt-hours (kWh). Simple circuit analysis: single-loop circuits with battery and resistors; use Ohm's law to find unknown quantities. Key concepts: conventional current direction (positive to negative), electron flow (negative to positive), complete circuit requirement, role of battery as energy source (maintains potential difference), electrical safety (grounding, fuses, circuit breakers). Vocabulary: Ohm's law, resistance, resistivity, current, voltage, power, energy, ohmic, non-ohmic, circuit, load, source, ground. Common misconceptions: larger resistors always dissipate more power; batteries supply constant current.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy including fluid dynamics and pressure systems
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers fluid mechanics for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should apply conservation principles to fluid systems. Fluid properties: density ρ = m/V, pressure P = F/A (measured in pascals, Pa). Pressure in fluids: increases with depth, P = P0 + ρgh (hydrostatic pressure); acts equally in all directions at a point (Pascal's principle). Pascal's principle: pressure applied to enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished (hydraulic systems: F1/A1 = F2/A2). Buoyancy: Archimedes' principle states the buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid: Fb = ρfluid × Vdisplaced × g. Object floats when Fb ≥ weight, sinks when Fb < weight. Fluid dynamics: equation of continuity A1v1 = A2v2 (conservation of mass for incompressible fluids); Bernoulli's equation P1 + ½ρv1² + ρgh1 = P2 + ½ρv2² + ρgh2 (conservation of energy for ideal fluids). Bernoulli's principle: where fluid speed increases, pressure decreases (airplane wings, carburetors, shower curtains). Vocabulary: density, pressure, pascal, buoyancy, Archimedes' principle, Pascal's principle, continuity equation, Bernoulli's equation, viscosity, laminar flow, turbulent flow, ideal fluid. Common misconceptions: heavy objects always sink; pressure only pushes down; Bernoulli's principle fully explains airplane lift.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy using work-energy theorem and thermal transformations
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers thermodynamics for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should apply conservation of energy principles to thermal systems. Temperature: measure of average translational kinetic energy of molecules; Kelvin scale (K = °C + 273.15). Heat: energy transferred due to temperature difference; flows from hot to cold. Internal energy: total kinetic and potential energy of all molecules in a system. First law of thermodynamics: ΔU = Q - W (change in internal energy = heat added minus work done by system). Work done by gas: W = PΔV (area under P-V curve). Thermodynamic processes: isothermal (constant T, ΔU = 0), isobaric (constant P), isochoric (constant V, W = 0), adiabatic (Q = 0). Heat transfer: conduction, convection, radiation. Specific heat: Q = mcΔT. Latent heat: Q = mL (phase changes at constant temperature). Thermal expansion: materials generally expand when heated. Ideal gas law: PV = nRT connects macroscopic properties. Key concepts: entropy increases in spontaneous processes, heat engines convert thermal energy to work, efficiency is always less than 100%. Vocabulary: temperature, heat, internal energy, entropy, specific heat, latent heat, isothermal, adiabatic, isobaric, isochoric, heat engine, efficiency. Common misconceptions: heat and temperature are the same; cold is a form of energy.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy including thermal energy transfer processes
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers heat transfer for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should understand thermal energy transfer processes and perform calculations. Three mechanisms: conduction (energy transfer through direct molecular collisions in solids; rate Q/t = kAΔT/L where k is thermal conductivity), convection (energy transfer by fluid movement; natural convection from density differences, forced convection from external means), radiation (energy transfer via electromagnetic waves; all objects emit thermal radiation; Stefan-Boltzmann law P = εσAT⁴). Thermal equilibrium: objects in contact reach same temperature; zeroth law of thermodynamics. Specific heat capacity: Q = mcΔT; water's high specific heat (4,184 J/kg·K) explains its role as climate moderator. Calorimetry: heat lost by hot object = heat gained by cold object (Qlost = Qgained) in an insulated system. Phase changes: energy absorbed or released without temperature change; Q = mLf (fusion) or Q = mLv (vaporization). Thermal conductors (metals) vs. insulators (wood, foam, air). R-value: measures insulation effectiveness (higher = better insulator). Vocabulary: conduction, convection, radiation, thermal conductivity, specific heat, calorimetry, latent heat, thermal equilibrium, insulator, conductor, emissivity, blackbody. Common misconceptions: metals are inherently cold; insulation adds heat; radiation requires a medium.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Calculate and explain work and power; identify when work occurs
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers work and power for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.7(A)). Students should calculate work and power and identify conditions under which work is performed. Work: W = Fd cos(θ), the product of force and displacement in the direction of force; measured in joules (J). Work is done only when a force causes displacement; no work if force is perpendicular to displacement (e.g., carrying a book horizontally — gravity does no work). Positive work: force component in direction of motion (speeds object up). Negative work: force component opposite to motion (slows object down, e.g., friction). Net work equals change in kinetic energy: Wnet = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mv0². Graphical analysis: work equals area under Force vs. displacement graph (including variable forces like springs). Power: P = W/t = Fv cos(θ), the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred; measured in watts (W). Instantaneous power: P = Fv. Key concepts: distinguish work done by individual forces from net work, work done by variable forces, energy efficiency and power ratings of machines and appliances. Vocabulary: work, joule, power, watt, net work, positive work, negative work, efficiency, force-displacement graph. Common misconceptions: all forces do work; more force always means more work; holding a weight requires work.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Analyze momentum conservation in inelastic and elastic collisions
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers conservation laws for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.7(E)). Students should analyze elastic and inelastic collisions using conservation of momentum and energy. Conservation of momentum: in an isolated system (no net external force), total momentum before interaction equals total momentum after: Σpi = Σpf. Applies to all collisions and explosions. Elastic collisions: both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved; objects bounce apart. For 1D elastic collision between equal masses, objects exchange velocities. Inelastic collisions: momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not (some converts to thermal energy, deformation, sound). Perfectly inelastic: objects stick together; m1v1 + m2v2 = (m1+m2)vf; maximum KE loss. Checking collision type: calculate KE before and after; if equal, elastic; if not, inelastic. Explosions: initially at rest system has total momentum = 0; after explosion, momenta of fragments sum to zero. Two-dimensional collisions: apply conservation of momentum separately in x and y directions. Combined conservation: when both energy and momentum are conserved, you can solve for two unknowns. Vocabulary: elastic collision, inelastic collision, perfectly inelastic, conservation of momentum, conservation of energy, isolated system, recoil, explosion. Common misconceptions: momentum is always conserved (only in isolated systems); elastic collisions are most common (most real collisions are inelastic).

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Investigate wave characteristics: velocity, frequency, amplitude, wavelength relationships
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers wave properties for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.8(C)). Students should investigate wave characteristics and the mathematical relationships connecting them. Fundamental wave properties: wavelength λ (distance between consecutive identical points), frequency f (oscillations per second, Hz), amplitude A (maximum displacement from equilibrium), period T = 1/f (time for one complete cycle). Wave equation: v = fλ, wave speed equals frequency times wavelength. Wave speed depends on the medium: string waves v = √(T/μ) where T is tension and μ is linear mass density; speed does not depend on frequency or amplitude. When a wave enters a new medium: speed and wavelength change, frequency stays the same. Energy and amplitude: wave energy is proportional to amplitude squared (E ∝ A²); intensity I = P/A decreases with distance from source (inverse square law for point sources). Wave types: transverse (displacement ⊥ to propagation) and longitudinal (displacement ∥ to propagation). Pulse behavior at boundaries: fixed end reflects inverted, free end reflects upright; partial reflection and transmission at media boundaries. Vocabulary: wavelength, frequency, amplitude, period, wave speed, transverse, longitudinal, pulse, medium, energy, intensity. Common misconceptions: wave speed depends on amplitude or frequency; the medium moves with the wave; higher frequency means higher speed.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Investigate wave behaviors including resonance, diffraction, and interference
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers resonance for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.8(D)). Students should understand resonance, diffraction, and interference phenomena. Resonance: occurs when a driving force has the same frequency as the system's natural frequency, resulting in maximum amplitude of oscillation. Natural frequency: the frequency at which a system oscillates when displaced and released; for mass-spring f = (1/2π)√(k/m), for pendulum f = (1/2π)√(g/L). Resonance examples: pushing a swing at its natural frequency, shattering glass with sound, tuning a radio to a station frequency, Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse. Standing waves as resonance: strings and air columns resonate at specific frequencies (harmonics). Diffraction: bending of waves around obstacles or through openings; more pronounced when opening size is comparable to wavelength. Single-slit diffraction: produces central maximum with decreasing-intensity side maxima. Interference combined with diffraction produces complex patterns. Damping affects resonance: underdamped systems oscillate with decreasing amplitude; critically damped return to equilibrium fastest; overdamped return slowly without oscillation. Quality factor Q describes sharpness of resonance peak. Vocabulary: resonance, natural frequency, driving frequency, damping, standing wave, harmonic, overtone, diffraction, constructive interference, quality factor. Common misconceptions: resonance always causes destruction; diffraction only happens with light; resonance creates energy.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Investigate wave behaviors including superposition and interference patterns
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers the principle of superposition for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.8(D)). Students should understand how waves combine and create interference patterns. Principle of superposition: when two or more waves overlap in the same medium, the resultant displacement at any point is the algebraic sum of the individual displacements. Constructive interference: waves in phase (crests align with crests); amplitudes add; results in larger displacement. Destructive interference: waves out of phase (crests align with troughs); amplitudes subtract; can result in zero displacement (complete cancellation for equal amplitudes). Standing waves: result from superposition of two identical waves traveling in opposite directions; characterized by nodes (zero displacement, destructive interference) and antinodes (maximum displacement, constructive interference). Standing waves on strings: boundary conditions determine allowed wavelengths; harmonics fn = nv/(2L) for fixed-fixed or open-open, fn = nv/(4L) for fixed-open (odd harmonics only). Beats: superposition of two waves with slightly different frequencies produces periodic variation in amplitude; beat frequency = |f1 - f2|. Vocabulary: superposition, constructive interference, destructive interference, standing wave, node, antinode, harmonic, fundamental frequency, overtone, beat frequency, phase. Common misconceptions: waves cancel each other permanently during destructive interference; superposition changes the waves themselves.

AP®︎/College Physics 1.teks_description: Investigate wave behaviors: reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference, Doppler effect
AP®︎/College Physics 1.prompt: This topic covers wave interference for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.8(D)). Students should understand reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference, and the Doppler effect as wave behaviors. Interference patterns: two-source interference produces bright (constructive) and dark (destructive) fringes. Path difference determines type: constructive when path difference = nλ (integer wavelengths), destructive when path difference = (n+½)λ. Double-slit experiment (Young's): d sin(θ) = mλ for maxima, demonstrates wave nature of light. Thin film interference: reflections from top and bottom surfaces interfere; phase shift of π upon reflection from higher-index medium. Reflection: angle of incidence equals angle of reflection; fixed-end reflection inverts wave, free-end does not. Refraction: waves change direction when entering media with different speeds; Snell's law n1 sin(θ1) = n2 sin(θ2). Diffraction: waves spread around obstacles and through openings; more diffraction when opening ≈ wavelength. Doppler effect: observed frequency changes due to relative motion; approaching source = higher frequency, receding = lower frequency; fo = fs(v ± vo)/(v ∓ vs). Polarization: restricting transverse wave oscillation to one plane; only transverse waves can be polarized. Vocabulary: interference, constructive, destructive, path difference, fringe, double-slit, diffraction, refraction, Doppler effect, polarization, thin film. Common misconceptions: interference permanently destroys waves; Doppler effect changes wave speed; refraction always produces a visible bend.

AP®︎/College Physics 2.teks_description: Predict electric force magnitude between objects using Coulomb's law
AP®︎/College Physics 2.prompt: This topic covers electrostatics and electric fields for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.6(A)). Students should analyze electric forces, fields, and potential in complex charge configurations. Coulomb's law: F = kq1q2/r², where k = 8.99 × 10^9 N·m²/C². Electric field: E = F/q = kQ/r² for point charges; field is a vector that represents force per unit positive charge. Field lines: originate on positive charges, terminate on negative charges; line density represents field strength; lines never cross. Superposition: net field at a point is the vector sum of fields from all charges. Gauss's law: ΦE = Q_enclosed/ε0; electric flux through a closed surface proportional to enclosed charge; useful for symmetric charge distributions (spherical, cylindrical, planar). Conductors in electrostatic equilibrium: E = 0 inside, excess charge on surface, field perpendicular to surface, strongest at points of highest curvature. Electric dipoles: pair of equal and opposite charges; dipole moment p = qd; experience torque in uniform fields and net force in nonuniform fields. Vocabulary: electric field, field lines, flux, Gauss's law, conductor, insulator, dipole, polarization, induced charge, electrostatic equilibrium, permittivity. Common misconceptions: electric field exists only between charges; field lines represent paths of moving charges.

AP®︎/College Physics 2.teks_description: Calculate current, potential difference, resistance, and power using Ohm's law
AP®︎/College Physics 2.prompt: This topic covers electric potential and capacitance for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.6(E)). Students should understand voltage, energy storage, and capacitor behavior. Electric potential: V = kQ/r for point charge; V = U/q (potential energy per unit charge); measured in volts (J/C). Potential difference: ΔV = -∫E·dr; work done per unit charge moving between two points; equipotential surfaces are perpendicular to field lines. Electric potential energy: U = kq1q2/r for point charges; U = qV. Capacitance: C = Q/V, ability to store charge; measured in farads (F). Parallel plate capacitor: C = ε0A/d (larger area and smaller separation increase capacitance). Energy stored: U = ½CV² = ½QV = Q²/(2C). Dielectrics: insulating materials between plates increase capacitance by factor κ (dielectric constant); C = κε0A/d. Capacitors in parallel: Ceq = C1 + C2 (same voltage, charges add). Capacitors in series: 1/Ceq = 1/C1 + 1/C2 (same charge, voltages add). Key concepts: relationship between E and V, E = -dV/dx, connecting circuits to field concepts. Vocabulary: electric potential, voltage, equipotential, capacitance, capacitor, dielectric, farad, energy density, parallel plate. Common misconceptions: voltage and current are the same; capacitors store current; larger capacitors always store more energy.

AP®︎/College Physics 2.teks_description: Identify electric and magnetic forces and fields in everyday applications
AP®︎/College Physics 2.prompt: This topic covers magnetic fields for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.6(B)). Students should understand magnetic field sources, forces on moving charges and currents, and applications. Magnetic field sources: moving charges, current-carrying wires, permanent magnets. Field from long straight wire: B = μ0I/(2πr), where μ0 = 4π × 10^-7 T·m/A. Field inside solenoid: B = μ0nI (uniform). Force on moving charge: F = qvB sin(θ), perpendicular to both v and B (right-hand rule); charged particles follow circular or helical paths in uniform fields with radius r = mv/(qB). Force on current-carrying wire: F = BIL sin(θ). Torque on current loop: τ = NIAB sin(θ); basis for electric motors. Ampere's law: ∮B·dl = μ0I_enclosed; useful for finding B with high symmetry. Magnetic materials: ferromagnetic (iron, domains align), paramagnetic (weakly attracted), diamagnetic (weakly repelled). Key concepts: right-hand rules for field direction and force direction, mass spectrometer operation, velocity selector (crossed E and B fields), Hall effect. Vocabulary: magnetic field, tesla, permeability, right-hand rule, solenoid, torque, domain, ferromagnetic, paramagnetic, diamagnetic, Ampere's law, Hall effect. Common misconceptions: magnetic force does work on charged particles (it changes direction only); all metals are magnetic.

AP®︎/College Physics 2.teks_description: Identify electromagnetic induction in generators, motors, and transformers
AP®︎/College Physics 2.prompt: This topic covers electromagnetic induction for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.6(B)). Students should understand Faraday's law, Lenz's law, and applications of electromagnetic induction. Magnetic flux: Φ = BA cos(θ), measured in webers (Wb). Faraday's law: induced emf = -NΔΦ/Δt; a changing magnetic flux induces an electromotive force. Ways to change flux: change B, change A, change θ, or move the loop relative to the field. Lenz's law: induced current flows in a direction to oppose the change in flux that caused it (conservation of energy). Motional emf: emf = BLv for a conductor of length L moving with velocity v perpendicular to field B. Applications: AC generators (rotating coil in magnetic field produces sinusoidal emf), transformers (Vs/Vp = Ns/Np; step-up increases voltage, step-down decreases), eddy currents (induced in bulk conductors, used for braking and metal detection). Self-inductance: L = NΦ/I; emf = -L(dI/dt); inductor opposes changes in current. Energy stored in inductor: U = ½LI². LC circuits: oscillation between electric and magnetic energy. Vocabulary: magnetic flux, Faraday's law, Lenz's law, induced emf, generator, transformer, eddy current, inductance, inductor, motional emf, weber. Common misconceptions: a static magnetic field induces current; transformers work with DC; induced current flows in the same direction as the change in flux.

AP®︎/College Physics 2.teks_description: Analyze and construct circuits using schematics including AC circuit analysis
AP®︎/College Physics 2.prompt: This topic covers AC circuits for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.6(D)). Students should analyze alternating current circuits with resistors, capacitors, and inductors. AC fundamentals: voltage varies sinusoidally V(t) = V0 sin(ωt); current also varies sinusoidally; frequency f = ω/(2π); typical household AC is 120 V RMS at 60 Hz. RMS values: Vrms = V0/√2, Irms = I0/√2; represent the equivalent DC values for power calculations. Impedance components: resistance R (voltage in phase with current), capacitive reactance XC = 1/(ωC) (current leads voltage by 90°), inductive reactance XL = ωL (current lags voltage by 90°). Impedance: Z = √(R² + (XL - XC)²); V = IZ. Phase angle: tan(φ) = (XL - XC)/R. Power: P = IrmsVrms cos(φ), where cos(φ) is the power factor. RLC series circuit: resonance occurs when XL = XC (impedance is minimum, current is maximum); resonant frequency f0 = 1/(2π√(LC)). Transformers: Vs/Vp = Ns/Np = Ip/Is; power is conserved (ideally). Key concepts: phasor diagrams, frequency response, bandwidth, quality factor. Vocabulary: alternating current, RMS, impedance, reactance, resonance, phasor, power factor, transformer, step-up, step-down, bandwidth, quality factor. Common misconceptions: AC and DC behave the same in circuits; capacitors and inductors dissipate power.

AP®︎/College Physics 2.teks_description: Compare transverse and longitudinal waves including sound waves
AP®︎/College Physics 2.prompt: This topic covers mechanical waves and sound for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.8(B)). Students should analyze wave behavior in depth, including standing waves and the Doppler effect. Wave fundamentals: mechanical waves require a medium; transverse (string, surface) vs. longitudinal (sound, spring compression). Wave equation: v = fλ. Sound: longitudinal pressure wave; speed depends on medium properties (vsound in air ≈ 343 m/s at 20°C, faster in denser media). Sound intensity: I = P/(4πr²) for point source (inverse-square law); intensity level β = 10 log(I/I0) in decibels, where I0 = 10^-12 W/m². Standing waves: superposition of incident and reflected waves. Strings (fixed-fixed): fn = nv/(2L), all harmonics. Open pipe: fn = nv/(2L), all harmonics. Closed pipe (one end closed): fn = nv/(4L), odd harmonics only. Beats: interference of waves with slightly different frequencies; fbeat = |f1 - f2|. Doppler effect: fo = fs(v ± vo)/(v ∓ vs); approaching = higher frequency, receding = lower frequency. Shock waves occur when source exceeds wave speed (Mach cone). Vocabulary: standing wave, harmonic, fundamental, overtone, node, antinode, resonance, beats, Doppler effect, intensity, decibel, Mach number, shock wave. Common misconceptions: sound travels fastest in air; higher harmonics are always louder; Doppler effect changes wave speed.

AP®︎/College Physics 2.teks_description: Describe image formation through reflection and refraction
AP®︎/College Physics 2.prompt: This topic covers ray and wave optics for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.8(G)). Students should understand image formation through reflection and refraction, and wave optics phenomena. Reflection: law of reflection (θi = θr). Plane mirrors: virtual, upright, same-size images. Curved mirrors: mirror equation 1/f = 1/do + 1/di; magnification M = -di/do; concave mirrors (f > 0) can form real or virtual images; convex mirrors (f < 0) always form virtual images. Refraction: Snell's law n1 sin(θ1) = n2 sin(θ2); total internal reflection when θ > θc = sin^-1(n2/n1). Lenses: thin lens equation 1/f = 1/do + 1/di; convex (converging, f > 0) and concave (diverging, f < 0). Wave optics: double-slit interference d sin(θ) = mλ (maxima); single-slit diffraction a sin(θ) = mλ (minima); thin film interference (phase changes upon reflection from higher-n medium). Diffraction gratings: d sin(θ) = mλ, sharper maxima than double slit. Resolution: Rayleigh criterion θmin = 1.22λ/D. Polarization: Malus's law I = I0 cos²(θ); Brewster's angle. Vocabulary: reflection, refraction, index of refraction, focal length, real image, virtual image, magnification, total internal reflection, diffraction, interference, polarization, Rayleigh criterion. Common misconceptions: lenses only form real images; virtual images cannot be seen; all light bends the same amount in refraction.

AP®︎/College Physics 2.teks_description: Describe photoelectric effect and emission spectra explained by photon model
AP®︎/College Physics 2.prompt: This topic covers quantum physics for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.9(A)). Students should understand the photon model and quantum phenomena. Photon model: light consists of discrete packets of energy (photons); E = hf = hc/λ, where h = 6.626 × 10^-34 J·s. Photoelectric effect: photons eject electrons from metal surface; KEmax = hf - φ (work function); threshold frequency f0 = φ/h; below f0, no electrons ejected regardless of intensity. Key observations explained by photon model: instantaneous emission above threshold, KE depends on frequency not intensity, number of electrons depends on intensity. Emission spectra: atoms emit photons at discrete frequencies when electrons transition between energy levels; Bohr model: En = -13.6/n² eV for hydrogen. Absorption spectra: atoms absorb photons matching energy level differences. Photon momentum: p = h/λ = hf/c. Compton scattering: photon-electron collision demonstrating particle nature of light. de Broglie wavelength: λ = h/mv; all matter has wave properties, significant only at atomic scale. Vocabulary: photon, photoelectric effect, work function, threshold frequency, emission spectrum, absorption spectrum, Bohr model, energy level, de Broglie wavelength, Compton scattering, wave-particle duality, quantization. Common misconceptions: brighter light always ejects electrons; electrons orbit nuclei like planets; photons have mass.

AP®︎/College Physics 2.teks_description: Provide examples of quantum and nuclear phenomena applications
AP®︎/College Physics 2.prompt: This topic covers atomic and nuclear physics for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.9(D)). Students should understand nuclear structure, radioactivity, and nuclear reactions. Nuclear structure: nucleus contains protons (Z) and neutrons (N); mass number A = Z + N; isotopes have same Z, different N. Nuclear forces: strong nuclear force (short-range, holds nucleons together, overcomes electromagnetic repulsion), weak nuclear force (responsible for beta decay). Mass-energy equivalence: E = mc²; mass defect Δm = (Zmp + Nmn) - mnucleus; binding energy Eb = Δmc². Binding energy per nucleon: peaks at iron-56 (most stable); explains why fission of heavy nuclei and fusion of light nuclei release energy. Radioactive decay: alpha (⁴₂He emission, Z decreases by 2, A by 4), beta-minus (neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino), beta-plus (proton → neutron + positron + neutrino), gamma (high-energy photon, no change in A or Z). Half-life: N = N0(½)^(t/t½); activity decreases exponentially. Fission: heavy nucleus splits (e.g., U-235 + neutron), chain reaction, nuclear reactors. Fusion: light nuclei combine (e.g., H → He in stars), requires extreme temperatures. Applications: nuclear power, medical imaging (PET, MRI), radiometric dating. Vocabulary: isotope, mass number, binding energy, mass defect, radioactive decay, half-life, alpha, beta, gamma, fission, fusion, chain reaction, activity. Common misconceptions: radioactive materials glow green; all radiation is harmful; fission and fusion are the same.

AP®︎/College Physics 2.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy using work-energy theorem and transformations
AP®︎/College Physics 2.prompt: This topic covers thermodynamics for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should apply conservation of energy and entropy concepts to thermodynamic systems. Laws of thermodynamics: Zeroth (thermal equilibrium is transitive, defines temperature), First (ΔU = Q - W, conservation of energy), Second (entropy of isolated system never decreases; heat flows spontaneously from hot to cold), Third (absolute zero is unattainable). Internal energy: U depends on temperature for ideal gas; ΔU = nCvΔT. Work by gas: W = ∫PdV, area under P-V curve. Thermodynamic processes: isothermal (constant T, ΔU = 0, Q = W), isobaric (constant P, W = PΔV), isochoric (constant V, W = 0, Q = ΔU), adiabatic (Q = 0, ΔU = -W). PV diagrams: represent processes and cycles; area enclosed = net work for cycles. Heat engines: convert heat to work; efficiency η = W/QH = 1 - QC/QH; Carnot efficiency ηC = 1 - TC/TH (maximum possible). Refrigerators/heat pumps: move heat from cold to hot, require work input; COP = QC/W. Entropy: S = Q/T for reversible processes; ΔS ≥ 0 for isolated systems. Vocabulary: internal energy, entropy, enthalpy, isothermal, isobaric, isochoric, adiabatic, heat engine, Carnot cycle, refrigerator, coefficient of performance, PV diagram. Common misconceptions: heat and work are forms of energy stored in a system; 100% efficient heat engines are possible; entropy means disorder.

AP®︎/College Physics 2.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy including fluid dynamics principles
AP®︎/College Physics 2.prompt: This topic covers fluid mechanics for AP Physics 2 students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should apply fluid statics and dynamics principles quantitatively. Fluid statics: pressure P = F/A, measured in pascals (Pa); hydrostatic pressure P = P0 + ρgh; pressure acts equally in all directions at a point. Pascal's principle: pressure change in enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished; hydraulic systems F1/A1 = F2/A2. Buoyancy: Archimedes' principle Fb = ρfluid × Vdisplaced × g; floating condition: object density < fluid density; fraction submerged = ρobject/ρfluid.
